·

Nov 16, 2025

[](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Fvote%2Fp%2F0a39560c52cb&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40gwrx2005%2Fanalyzing-the-technical-stack-of-synthetic-dollar-stablecoins-0a39560c52cb&user=Jung-Hua+Liu&userId=6552dfe1e986&source=---header_actions--0a39560c52cb---------------------clap_footer------------------)

7

[](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Fbookmark%2Fp%2F0a39560c52cb&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40gwrx2005%2Fanalyzing-the-technical-stack-of-synthetic-dollar-stablecoins-0a39560c52cb&source=---header_actions--0a39560c52cb---------------------bookmark_footer------------------)

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3D0a39560c52cb&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40gwrx2005%2Fanalyzing-the-technical-stack-of-synthetic-dollar-stablecoins-0a39560c52cb&source=---header_actions--0a39560c52cb---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

**Abstract:**

 Stablecoins have become a cornerstone of the cryptocurrency economy, offering price stability amid volatility. A new class of _synthetic dollar_ stablecoins has emerged that uses financial engineering — rather than solely fiat reserves — to maintain a U.S. dollar peg while generating yield for holders. This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the technical stack underpinning synthetic dollar stablecoins, using **Ethena USDe** as a primary case study. We examine the financial mechanisms (delta‐neutral hedging and basis trading), on-chain and off-chain architecture, risk management approaches, and economic implications of these protocols. Our interdisciplinary analysis spans finance (yield generation and market impact), computer science (system architecture and oracle design), blockchain/DeFi (smart contracts and integration with exchanges), and economics (market adoption and stability). We find that synthetic dollar stablecoins like USDe combine **decentralized on-chain components** (smart contracts for token issuance and staking) with **centralized off-chain infrastructure** (exchange-traded derivatives and custodial services) to achieve a 1:1 USD peg. The stablecoin’s stability and yield derive from **delta-neutral hedging strategies**: user deposits of volatile crypto assets are counterbalanced by short positions in perpetual futures, yielding funding rate income without net market exposure[_[1]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=At%20a%20basic%20level%2C%20every,neutral%20hedge)[_[2]_](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2024/05/07/ethenas-ena-jumps-8-as-bybit-endorses-usde-token-as-collateral-for-derivatives-trading#:~:text=The%20protocol%27s%20USDe%20token%2C%20often,funding%20rates%20for%20a%20yield). This innovative architecture effectively tokenizes a traditional “cash-and-carry” basis trade, allowing stablecoin holders to earn a share of trading revenues that would otherwise be accessible only to hedge funds or institutional investors[_[3]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Ethena%20aims%20to%20provide%20a,traditional%20finance%3A%20the%20basis%20trade)[_[4]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Among%20the%20new%20entrants%2C%20Ethena,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months). We also discuss the **risk trade-offs** of this model: reliance on external exchanges and custodians introduces counterparty and liquidity risks, and the dual mandate of stability _and_ yield creates tension that must be managed through robust governance and technical safeguards[_[5]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=Insight%203%3A%20The%20integration%20of,fundamentally%20redefines%20the%20asset%E2%80%99s%20evolutionary)[_[6]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=and%20liquidity%20for%20hedging,funding%20rates). Empirically, Ethena’s USDe grew to a circulation of ~$15 billion within two years (becoming the third-largest stablecoin) before a stress event in late 2025 tested its resilience[_[7]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=While%20UXD%20Protocol%20was%20ahead,overall%2C%20after%20USDC%20and%20USDT)[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions). Looking ahead, the expansion of perpetual futures to tokenized real-world assets (“perpification”) and broader DeFi integration could significantly increase the capacity and impact of synthetic dollar stablecoins[_[9]_](https://info.arkm.com/research/how-stablecoins-reached-a-300-billion-market-cap-in-2025#:~:text=The%20big%20change%20this%20year,of%20the%20market)[_[10]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Perpetuals%20on%20Tokenized%20Equities). Our analysis concludes that synthetic dollar stablecoins represent a compelling fusion of **financial engineering and blockchain technology**, potentially reshaping the stablecoin landscape by offering higher yields and new use cases — but only if their complex risk factors are carefully managed.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 3](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*t1TALFbzeATbcvGhih-g9A.png)

## Introduction

Stablecoins are digital assets designed to maintain a stable value (typically pegged to fiat currencies like the U.S. dollar) and serve as a bridge between volatile cryptocurrencies and traditional money[_[11]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=Digital%20assets%2C%20particularly%20cryptocurrencies%2C%20offer,market%20fluctuations%20and%20economic%20turbulence). Over the past several years, stablecoins have grown into a significant asset class, with a total market capitalization exceeding \$246 billion by mid-2025[_[12]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=Stablecoins%20have%20become%20significant%20assets,and%2044%20major%20security%20incidents) and over \$300 billion by late 2025[_[13]_](https://info.arkm.com/research/how-stablecoins-reached-a-300-billion-market-cap-in-2025#:~:text=%E2%80%8D). This rapid growth underscores stablecoins’ critical role in cryptocurrency trading, DeFi applications, and as an alternative store of value in countries with unstable local currencies[_[14]_](https://research.grayscale.com/reports/holding-steady-the-rise-of-stablecoins#:~:text=So%2C%20why%20do%20stablecoins%E2%80%99%20value,continue%20to%20grow)[_[15]_](https://www.reuters.com/technology/stablecoin-tether-gets-boost-dollar-alternative-emerging-markets-ceo-says-2024-04-19/#:~:text=,life%20in%20the%20desert%20city). Yet, the stablecoin market has been dominated by a _duopoly_ of two issuers — **Tether’s USDT** and **Circle’s USDC** — which together accounted for more than 80% of all stablecoin supply as of 2023[_[16]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=There%20are%20over%20%24300%20billion,norm%20rather%20than%20the%20exception). These leading stablecoins are fully backed by traditional financial assets (cash and short-term treasuries) and have largely maintained their pegs through transparency and reserve management[_[17]_](https://research.grayscale.com/reports/holding-steady-the-rise-of-stablecoins#:~:text=that%20they%20maintain%20a%20reserve,money%20market%20funds%2C%20and%20other). However, they characteristically **do not share yield** with holders, meaning users earn no interest on holding USDT or USDC[_[18]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=trillions%20by%20the%20end%20of,norm%20rather%20than%20the%20exception). The foregone interest (e.g. from U.S. Treasury bills) has instead accrued to the issuing companies, a model which has become increasingly scrutinized as interest rates and crypto yields have risen in recent years.

In response, a new generation of stablecoins is exploring ways to **offer yield** to holders while maintaining a stable peg. These designs range from fully reserve-backed stablecoins that distribute interest from treasuries, to more complex “algorithmic” or “crypto-collateralized” stablecoins that utilize other mechanisms. We can broadly classify stablecoins into two categories[_[19]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=We%20divide%20stablecoin%20as%20a,category%20into%20two%20parts): (1) those that _do not_ distribute any yield (e.g. USDT, USDC), and (2) those that _do_ share yield with users. Among yield-bearing stablecoins, a further distinction exists between (a) stablecoins entirely backed by low-risk, government-backed assets (like tokenized treasuries) and (b) those that are **synthetic**, meaning they **are not fully backed 1:1 by safe assets** but instead generate returns through active strategies[_[20]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=1,Ones%20that%20aren%E2%80%99t%3A%20Synthetic%20dollars). It is this latter class that we term **“synthetic dollars” (合成美元)** — stablecoins that aim to maintain a $1.00 value via financial engineering (e.g. hedging and arbitrage strategies) rather than a traditional reserve of cash or treasuries[_[21]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Synthetic%20Dollars%20are%20not%20entirely,trading%20strategies%20in%20financial%20markets).

A prominent example is **Ethena USD** (**USDe**), which has quickly become the largest synthetic dollar stablecoin and a case study for this paper. Launched in 2024, Ethena’s USDe reached a circulating supply of over \$14–15 billion at its peak (around 5% of the entire stablecoin market), surpassing DAI to become the third-largest stablecoin behind USDT and USDC[_[9]_](https://info.arkm.com/research/how-stablecoins-reached-a-300-billion-market-cap-in-2025#:~:text=The%20big%20change%20this%20year,of%20the%20market)[_[7]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=While%20UXD%20Protocol%20was%20ahead,overall%2C%20after%20USDC%20and%20USDT). Unlike USDT/USDC which hold fiat reserves, USDe’s **peg and yield are maintained by a delta-neutral trading strategy** rather than by dollar collateral[_[22]_](https://info.arkm.com/research/how-stablecoins-reached-a-300-billion-market-cap-in-2025#:~:text=USDe%E2%80%99s%20rise%20is%20significant%20because%2C,and%20USDT%20to%20remain%20liquid). In simple terms, Ethena operates a **crypto-native hedge fund within the stablecoin protocol**: when users mint USDe by depositing crypto assets, the system opens equal and opposite short positions on those assets’ perpetual futures markets, so that the net value remains stable regardless of price movements[_[1]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=At%20a%20basic%20level%2C%20every,neutral%20hedge)[_[23]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,%E2%86%92%20net%20value%20%E2%89%88%20%24100). This structure allows USDe to remain **approximately pegged to \$1** even as the underlying collateral values fluctuate, because gains/losses on the collateral are offset by losses/gains on the short futures (a _delta-neutral_ position). Moreover, by holding this hedged position, the protocol continuously earns **funding rate payments** from traders longing the futures contract, which provides the yield distributed to USDe holders who stake their tokens[_[24]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=The%20stablecoin%20value%20holds%20steady,all%20these%20mechanics%20later%20on)[_[25]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=This%20is%20how%20it%20works,on%20example). Essentially, USDe tokenizes the returns of a well-known arbitrage in traditional finance called the **basis trade**, capturing the spread between futures and spot prices (or the perp funding rates) as revenue[_[3]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Ethena%20aims%20to%20provide%20a,traditional%20finance%3A%20the%20basis%20trade)[_[26]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=sUSDe%E2%80%99s%20price%20change%20since%20inception%2C,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months).

This paper delves into the **technical stack** that makes such a synthetic stablecoin possible. We begin with background on stablecoin designs and the motivations for synthetic dollars, including the limitations of earlier models (like over-collateralized or purely algorithmic stablecoins). We then dissect the architecture of Ethena’s USDe: its on-chain smart contracts, off-chain hedging engine, oracle system, and the integration with centralized exchanges (CEXs) and custodians that facilitate its core strategy. We analyze how the system manages stability (the peg) and distributes yield, and how it has performed during both normal market conditions and extreme stress events. Key **risk factors** — such as dependency on perpetual futures liquidity and exchange counterparty risk — are examined in light of real incidents (e.g. the Bybit exchange hack and the October 2025 crypto crash) to evaluate the robustness of the design[_[27]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Daily%2C%20profits%20and%20losses%20,assets%20remain%20with%20the%20custodian)[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions). Finally, we consider the economic and technological implications of synthetic dollar stablecoins. From an economics perspective, yield-bearing stablecoins could transform stablecoin adoption and competition; indeed, J.P. Morgan analysts project that yield-bearing stablecoins could grow from just ~6% of the market today to as much as **50%** in coming years[_[28]_](https://k33.com/research/articles/cap-a-new-stablecoin-frontier#:~:text=%2A%20Yield,ecosystem%3A%20minters%2C%20operators%2C%20and%20restakers)[_[29]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=funding%20rates%20app,rates). From a technological standpoint, Ethena’s model represents a convergence of DeFi and traditional markets — hinting at future expansion into tokenized real-world assets and deeper integration with fintech platforms.

In summary, synthetic dollar stablecoins like Ethena USDe illustrate a novel approach to stablecoin design — one that **blends financial engineering with blockchain infrastructure** to achieve a stable, interest-bearing asset. This approach holds great promise in addressing the “final frontier” of stablecoins (offering competitive yield to users)[_[30]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=In%20our%20blogpost%2C%20The%20Endgame,market%20size%20for%20synthetic%20dollars)[_[31]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Among%20the%20new%20entrants%2C%20Ethena,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months), but it also introduces new complexities and risks. Our analysis provides a detailed, multi-faceted understanding of how the technical stack of synthetic dollars works, why it has gained traction, and what challenges remain for it to sustainably rival the incumbent stablecoin models.

## Background: Stablecoin Designs and the Rise of Synthetic Dollars

To contextualize the synthetic dollar model, it is useful to survey the landscape of stablecoin designs and their respective trade-offs. Existing stablecoins can be categorized by **how they maintain their peg** and whether they generate yield for holders:

*   **Fiat-Backed Stablecoins (Centralized Reserves):** These are issued by a central entity and fully backed by traditional financial assets, typically bank deposits, Treasury bills, or similar cash equivalents. Examples include USDT (Tether) and USDC (Circle). They maintain stability through 1:1 redeemability for fiat currency and conservative reserve management. These stablecoins generally _do not_ pay interest to holders — even though reserves earn yield (e.g. USDT and USDC issuers invest in short-term T-bills yielding around 5% in 2023–2024) — meaning the issuers retain the revenue[_[3]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Ethena%20aims%20to%20provide%20a,traditional%20finance%3A%20the%20basis%20trade)[_[32]_](https://sharonyross.com/GKRRV_LeverageAndStablecoinPegs.pdf#:~:text=and%20money%20market%20funds,compensated%20for%20holding%20risky%20stablecoins). Fiat-backed coins offer high stability and liquidity but rely on trust in the issuer’s solvency and transparency. They also concentrate systemic risk: for instance, the top two (USDT & USDC) at one point made up over 80% of stablecoin supply[_[16]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=There%20are%20over%20%24300%20billion,norm%20rather%20than%20the%20exception) and their combined market cap was larger than that of the next 10 stablecoin projects _combined_[_[33]_](https://research.grayscale.com/reports/holding-steady-the-rise-of-stablecoins#:~:text=match%20at%20L238%20investments%20,8). This dominance underscores a risk of duopoly and single points of failure in stablecoin infrastructure.
*   **Crypto-Collateralized Stablecoins (Over-Collateralized DeFi Models):** These stablecoins, exemplified by MakerDAO’s **DAI**, are backed by cryptocurrency collateral locked in smart contracts. Users mint the stablecoin by depositing assets (ETH, etc.) worth more than the amount being issued, creating an over-collateralized loan. Automated on-chain mechanisms (or keeper bots) **liquidate** collateral if its value falls too low, thereby maintaining solvency and the peg[_[34]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=match%20at%20L701%20,C%203%20Hedging)[_[35]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=4%20%20%20Ethena%20Labs,Fiat%20currency%20%20%20Implicit). DAI, for example, is typically 150% or more collateralized and uses market-driven auctions to sell collateral for DAI if needed to uphold the $1 peg. While decentralized and transparent, this model is capital-inefficient (requiring excess collateral) and its stability depends on volatile crypto markets and timely liquidations. Historically, DAI and similar coins have maintained stability by erring on the side of over-collateralization and even incorporating elements of centralized collateral (Maker has included USDC in reserves) to mitigate extreme volatility. These coins also generally do not provide yield to holders by default (unless holders actively lend them out in DeFi). The cost of holding is effectively the opportunity cost of the over-collateralized assets.
*   **Algorithmic Stablecoins (Seigniorage and Elastic Supply Models):** These designs attempt to maintain a peg via algorithmic expansion and contraction of supply, often without full collateral backing. A famous example was TerraUSD (UST), which relied on a dual-token mechanism with LUNA: the protocol would mint or burn UST and LUNA to push UST’s price toward $1. In theory, arbitrage incentives (1 UST redeemable for \$1 of LUNA) would stabilize the price[_[36]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=Case%20Study%3A%20Terra%20UST%2FLUNA,academic%20discussion%20of%20this%20vulnerability). Some algorithmic models also enticed users with very high yields (such as Anchor Protocol’s 20% APY on UST) to drive demand. However, purely algorithmic stablecoins proved **fragile and prone to collapse**, as demonstrated by UST’s dramatic failure in May 2022. Once confidence faltered, a death spiral ensued — UST fell below peg, new UST was minted by burning LUNA (cratering LUNA’s price), which led to further UST devaluation in a vicious cycle[_[36]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=Case%20Study%3A%20Terra%20UST%2FLUNA,academic%20discussion%20of%20this%20vulnerability)[_[37]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=The%20sheer%20scale%20of%20the,attacks%20or%20cascading%20internal%20breakdowns). The Terra/LUNA crash wiped out ~$40–60 billion in value and highlighted the extreme risk of under-collateralized, reflexive stablecoin designs[_[38]_](https://research.grayscale.com/reports/holding-steady-the-rise-of-stablecoins#:~:text=operative%20word,collapse%20of%20the%20Terra%2FLuna%20ecosystem)[_[39]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=The%20sheer%20scale%20of%20the,against%20economic%20attacks%20or%20cascading). Academic analyses have since dissected this event, noting that many warnings about structural vulnerabilities went unheeded[_[40]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=The%20sheer%20scale%20of%20the,against%20economic%20attacks%20or%20cascading). Algorithmic stablecoins, in general, have struggled to achieve lasting stability and are regarded as highly risky unless supplemented by collateral or other mechanisms.
*   **Hybrid and Other Models:** Some stablecoins use fractional reserve models or novel mechanisms (e.g. **Ampleforth** with rebasing supply, **FRAX** with partial collateral + algorithmic elements). Another emerging category is stablecoins **backed by tokenized real-world assets (RWAs)** like tokenized bank deposits or bonds; for example, Coinbase’s upcoming stablecoin plans or projects like **Ondo’s USDY** which tokenize yields from traditional assets. These typically fall under yield-bearing fiat-backed stablecoins.

Within the yield-bearing cohort, **Synthetic Stablecoins** (合成美元) have recently gained attention as a distinct approach. These protocols maintain their peg through _active trading strategies_ rather than static reserves. They often **share the generated yield with users**, aligning with the crypto ethos of decentralization and user empowerment. The concept was pioneered by projects like **UXD Protocol** in 2021, which issued a Solana-based stablecoin (UXD) fully collateralized by a delta-neutral position (long crypto + short perpetual futures)[_[41]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=As%20a%20team%2C%20we%E2%80%99ve%20been,backed%20by%20a%20basis%20trade). UXD demonstrated the viability of the basis trade mechanism but struggled with timing and market fit (it launched just before a bear market and eventually faced liquidity challenges)[_[42]_](https://www.theblock.co/post/311950/solana-based-uxd-protocols-dao-is-voting-on-whether-to-sunset-its-stablecoin#:~:text=Solana,The). Learning from such predecessors, Ethena’s **USDe** refined the model and launched on Ethereum in 2024, when market conditions and infrastructure were more favorable. By late 2024, USDe had already grown past \$5 billion in supply[_[35]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=4%20%20%20Ethena%20Labs,Fiat%20currency%20%20%20Implicit) and was accepted as collateral on major exchanges, signaling significant adoption. By mid-2025 it reached ~\$12–15 billion supply, before volatility in Q4 2025 reduced it to about \$8–9 billion[_[7]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=While%20UXD%20Protocol%20was%20ahead,overall%2C%20after%20USDC%20and%20USDT)[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions).

**What sets synthetic stablecoins apart** is that they are effectively **“tokenized hedge funds” or “ETF-like” products** packaged as stablecoins[_[43]_](https://decrypt.co/116145/blackrock-ceo-says-next-generationmarkets-is-tokenization#:~:text=Discover%20SCENE)[_[2]_](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2024/05/07/ethenas-ena-jumps-8-as-bybit-endorses-usde-token-as-collateral-for-derivatives-trading#:~:text=The%20protocol%27s%20USDe%20token%2C%20often,funding%20rates%20for%20a%20yield). Traditional ETFs often wrap a complex strategy (e.g. a basket of assets or a futures-based exposure) into a single tradeable share; analogously, a synthetic stablecoin wraps a complex market-neutral strategy into a token redeemable for \$1. Ethena’s USDe, for instance, can be seen as a **structured product** — it uses a combination of **staked ETH** (yield-bearing collateral) and **short ETH perp positions** to create a synthetic dollar that yields interest[_[2]_](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2024/05/07/ethenas-ena-jumps-8-as-bybit-endorses-usde-token-as-collateral-for-derivatives-trading#:~:text=The%20protocol%27s%20USDe%20token%2C%20often,funding%20rates%20for%20a%20yield). The tokenization of this strategy has two major benefits:

1.   **Stability:** The delta-neutral design aims to keep the net asset value of the collateral equal to the outstanding stablecoin liabilities, thus maintaining a stable peg. If ETH’s price rises, the value of deposited ETH goes up but the short position incurs losses of equal magnitude; if ETH’s price falls, the collateral value drops but the short position yields gains — in both cases, the **net portfolio remains approximately constant** (ignoring second-order effects like funding payments)[_[25]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=This%20is%20how%20it%20works,on%20example). By continually rebalancing to maintain Δ ≈ 0 (zero net exposure), the protocol can ensure each USDe is backed by ~$1 worth of net assets[_[1]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=At%20a%20basic%20level%2C%20every,neutral%20hedge).
2.   **Yield Generation:** Unlike a fiat-backed stablecoin whose backing assets (cash or T-bills) yield interest in a bank account, a synthetic stablecoin’s backing assets actively **earn trading income**. In Ethena’s model, the primary source of yield is the **funding rate** paid by perpetual futures long traders to the short side[_[44]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,it%20further%20in%20this%20article)[_[45]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=We%20identified%208%20primary%20yield,native%20risk%20appetites). In crypto futures markets, when perpetual contracts trade above the spot price (indicating bullish leverage demand), longs pay shorts a periodic fee (funding) to tether the contract to the underlying price[_[46]_](https://sharonyross.com/GKRRV_LeverageAndStablecoinPegs.pdf#:~:text=Figure%203%3A%20Perpetual%20Futures%20Funding,future). Ethena basically takes the short side of this trade at scale, collecting those funding fees as revenue. Additionally, Ethena stakes the collateral (if it’s a PoS asset like ETH) to earn staking rewards, and it can allocate a portion of reserves to other yield opportunities (like lending out USD or holding tokenized T-bills) when advantageous[_[47]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=1,execution%20layer%20rewards)[_[48]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=3,Stables). All these revenue streams are aggregated and (after protocol fees or reserves) distributed to users who stake USDe (holding it as **sUSDe** receipt tokens). In effect, USDe holders earn yields sourced from real economic activity — margin trading demand and blockchain consensus rewards — rather than from any kind of Ponzi-like issuance or purely internal incentives. This stands in contrast to some earlier “high-yield” stablecoins that relied on unsustainable token subsidies or community funds to pay interest[_[49]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=3,based%20tokens%20devalue%20under%20pressure).

It’s important to note that **yield-bearing stablecoins introduce a “dual mandate”** of stability and return, which is a delicate balance. As highlighted by recent research, over half of stablecoins now attempt to offer yield, often exceeding U.S. Treasury rates, which forces them into higher-risk strategies (derivatives, DeFi lending, etc.) to stay competitive[_[5]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=Insight%203%3A%20The%20integration%20of,fundamentally%20redefines%20the%20asset%E2%80%99s%20evolutionary). This fundamentally changes the risk profile of the stablecoin sector: stablecoins are no longer passive value anchors but **actively managed financial instruments** with attendant complexities[_[50]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=2,solvency%20issues%20across%20multiple%20stablecoins)[_[5]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=Insight%203%3A%20The%20integration%20of,fundamentally%20redefines%20the%20asset%E2%80%99s%20evolutionary). The synthetic dollar approach is perhaps the most sophisticated instantiation of this trend, requiring expertise in both smart contract development and Wall Street-style risk management. In the following sections, we dissect how Ethena Labs engineered the USDe stablecoin’s technical stack to meet these challenges.

## Technical Architecture of Ethena’s Synthetic Dollar (USDe)

Ethena’s USDe stablecoin is built on a **hybrid architecture** that combines on-chain smart contracts and off-chain systems in a tightly coordinated manner[_[51]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Ethena%20combines%20on,liquidity%20and%20delta%20neutral%20hedging). The design reflects the need to straddle decentralized and centralized components: the stablecoin must be trustless and transparent enough to satisfy blockchain users, yet it must interface with centralized exchanges and custodians to execute the hedging strategy at scale. Figure 1 illustrates the high-level architecture of Ethena USDe, which we will detail component by component (for clarity, “on-chain” refers to Ethereum smart contracts, while “off-chain” refers to the backend services and exchange integrations):

_Fig. 1: Circulating supply of Ethena USDe over time (Feb 2024 — Oct 2025). USDe grew rapidly after launch, surpassing DAI to become the third-largest USD stablecoin by mid-2025, with a peak circulation around \$15 billion. A sharp deleveraging event on Oct. 10–11, 2025 caused the supply to contract (~45% drop) as some users redeemed USDe amid market stress_[_[7]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=While%20UXD%20Protocol%20was%20ahead,overall%2C%20after%20USDC%20and%20USDT)[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions)_. Despite the turbulence, USDe’s on-chain pools largely held the peg, highlighting the protocol’s resilience under stress._

## On-Chain Components and Smart Contracts

On Ethereum, Ethena deploys a suite of smart contracts that handle the core stablecoin issuance logic, staking, and governance[_[52]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=On)[_[53]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=restricted%20to%20whitelisted%20addresses%3B%20redemptions,affect%20USDe%20or%20sUSDe%20mechanics). The key contracts include:

*   **USDe Token Contract (ERC-20):** This contract governs the minting and burning of USDe tokens. It enforces that only authorized accounts (a whitelist of minters) can mint new USDe, and it emits events (Transfer, Mint, Redeem) for every token movement[_[54]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Core%20Contracts). When a user initiates a redemption of USDe back to collateral, the contract burns the returned USDe and signals the off-chain system to release the corresponding collateral (more on this process later). By watching these events, the off-chain hedging engine knows when to adjust positions.
*   **sUSDe Staking Contract:** USDe holders can stake their tokens into this contract to receive **sUSDe**, a staked token that accrues yield. Internally, the staking contract locks USDe and keeps track of each user’s share of the reward pool[_[55]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,affect%20USDe%20or%20sUSDe%20mechanics). When users unstake, they burn sUSDe and get back their USDe plus any accumulated yield (payout). The staking process is atomic and synchronized with off-chain accounting to prevent any mismatch between what the on-chain contract thinks is owed and what the off-chain hedge has generated. Essentially, the sUSDe contract functions like a high-yield savings account where deposits/withdrawals are tokenized.
*   **ENA Governance Token and Fee Mechanisms:** Ethena also has a governance token, ENA, which is used for protocol governance decisions (such as risk parameters or fee switches)[_[56]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,affect%20USDe%20or%20sUSDe%20mechanics). While ENA doesn’t directly affect the peg mechanics of USDe, it is relevant for the long-term value capture of the system. As of 2025, Ethena has proposed a “fee switch” where a portion of protocol revenues (currently all going to USDe stakers) would be diverted to ENA stakers or used for buy-and-burn, once certain growth milestones are met[_[57]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=twice%20that%20of%20treasury,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months). This incentivizes community governance and aligns long-term stakeholders. However, details of ENA’s economics are outside our present scope, which focuses on the stablecoin’s technical operation.

Supporting these contracts, Ethena’s on-chain system also includes modules for configuration and emergency control (e.g. the ability to pause minting in extreme scenarios), and it emits a variety of events that allow external observers or dashboards to track the system’s state in real time[_[58]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Ethena%E2%80%99s%20on,chain%20systems%20to%20react)[_[59]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=handling%20a%20distinct%20part%20of,chain%20systems%20to%20react). Notably, Ethena conducts monthly **Proof-of-Reserve audits** to verify on-chain that each USDe is fully backed by appropriate collateral, adding transparency for users[_[60]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=The%20%E2%80%9Chedge%20fund%E2%80%9D%20is%20fully,transparent).

## Off-Chain Hedging Engine and Custodial Infrastructure

While the on-chain contracts manage user interactions and bookkeeping, the heavy lifting of maintaining the peg is done by Ethena’s **off-chain hedging engine**. This is a sophisticated automated system that connects to multiple centralized exchanges (such as Binance, Bybit, OKX) and possibly decentralized derivatives exchanges, to execute the needed trades for delta-neutral hedging[_[51]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Ethena%20combines%20on,liquidity%20and%20delta%20neutral%20hedging)[_[61]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=%2A%20An%20off,with%20multiple%20exchanges%20and%20custodians). The main responsibilities of the off-chain engine include:

*   **Price Feeds and Oracles:** To determine how much collateral to take or return for each USDe, the system needs accurate real-time prices. Ethena employs a _multi-layered oracle mechanism_ for robustness[_[62]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Oracles%20networks)[_[63]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=%2A%20On,of%20CeFi%20and%20DeFi%20markets). First, the off-chain engine subscribes to live market data feeds (order books, trade prices, index prices) from all exchanges where it operates[_[64]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=%2A%20Exchange%20Feeds%20%28Off,what%20each%20position%20is%20worth). These direct exchange feeds reflect the actual execution prices and funding rates that Ethena will encounter. Second, Ethena leverages decentralized on-chain oracles — specifically from Pyth Network and RedStone — which publish aggregated price feeds for major assets (BTC, ETH, etc.) on Ethereum[_[65]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=deep%20dive%20). Before any mint or redeem is processed, the system cross-verifies the exchange prices with the on-chain oracle values, and also runs them through an **internal pricing engine** that checks for consistency and slippage[_[66]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Before%20any%20mint%20or%20redeem,prices%20differ%2C%20the%20operation%20stops). If any source deviates significantly, the operation is halted, preventing a single exchange’s faulty data from corrupting the system[_[67]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=frequency%2C%20sourced%20from%20a%20mix,of%20CeFi%20and%20DeFi%20markets)[_[66]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Before%20any%20mint%20or%20redeem,prices%20differ%2C%20the%20operation%20stops). This defense is crucial to avoid exploitation via price manipulation on a thin market. In summary, Ethena’s oracle design prioritizes **data integrity** by requiring agreement across multiple sources and by halting on anomalies, thereby mitigating oracle attacks or outlier events.
*   **Collateral Custody and Exchange Interface:** One of the ingenious aspects of Ethena’s setup is how it manages collateral on exchanges without fully trusting the exchanges. Ethena uses regulated third-party **custodians** to hold the collateral assets (e.g. the ETH or BTC that users deposit) in segregated accounts[_[68]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Off). These custodians then provide the exchanges with permission to trade against those funds (often via API integration or a “credit line” approach), but **the assets themselves remain under custodian control**[_[69]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Ethena%20uses%20third,full%20control%20of%20the%20collateral). This arrangement is often called _Off-Exchange Settlement (OES)_ or similar. It means if an exchange were to fail or freeze withdrawals, Ethena’s assets are not trapped on the exchange — only the unsettled profits/losses for that trading period would be affected[_[70]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Daily%2C%20profits%20and%20losses%20,assets%20remain%20with%20the%20custodian). In practice, Ethena reconciles PnL with the custodian daily. If the trading on Exchange X generated a profit, that profit is moved into the custodian account (increasing collateral); if there was a loss, the custodian will transfer some collateral to the exchange to meet margin calls[_[70]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Daily%2C%20profits%20and%20losses%20,assets%20remain%20with%20the%20custodian). By splitting positions across **multiple exchanges** and limiting exposure to roughly one day’s worth of PnL at each, Ethena dramatically reduces the risk that any single exchange failure could bankrupt the stablecoin[_[71]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Ethena%E2%80%99s%20exposure%20is%20roughly%20one,protections%20to%20keep%20USDe%20hedged)[_[72]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Positions%20are%20split%20across%20multiple,tied%20to%20multiple%20CeFi%20venues). For example, when the FTX exchange collapsed in 2022, many trading firms lost all funds stuck on FTX. Ethena’s design would have shielded most of the funds in custodian accounts off the exchange, incurring only minimal losses (if any) for that venue and allowing hedges to be reopened elsewhere[_[71]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Ethena%E2%80%99s%20exposure%20is%20roughly%20one,protections%20to%20keep%20USDe%20hedged)[_[73]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=reserve,protections%20to%20keep%20USDe%20hedged). This _distributed counterparty risk_ approach is a key risk management innovation in Ethena’s stack.
*   **Delta-Neutral Hedging Operations:** The core function of the off-chain engine is to continuously maintain the delta-neutral hedge for all outstanding USDe. Whenever new USDe is minted, the engine must **short an equivalent amount** of the underlying asset’s perpetual futures; conversely, when USDe is redeemed and collateral withdrawn, the engine must **close out** the corresponding short position. This requires fast, automated trade execution. The engine performs continuous **risk monitoring**, calculating the net exposure (delta) across all positions and all exchanges, and issuing trades whenever needed to correct any imbalance[_[74]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,prevent%20minting%20or%20redeeming%20if)[_[75]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,notify%20engineers%20of%20any%20issues). For instance, if Alice deposits 100 ETH worth \$200,000 to mint 200,000 USDe, the engine might split the required \$200k ETH short across Binance and Bybit (say \$100k short each) to hedge the new exposure. If later Bob redeems 50,000 USDe for ETH, the engine will reduce the short positions accordingly (buying back some ETH perps). The system also accounts for **funding rate optimization** — it can allocate hedges to whichever exchange is offering the most favorable funding (highest payment to shorts) as long as liquidity is sufficient[_[72]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Positions%20are%20split%20across%20multiple,tied%20to%20multiple%20CeFi%20venues). By spreading positions across venues, Ethena maximizes yield capture while minimizing any one venue’s costs or risks. The backend runs 24/7 like a high-frequency trading platform, with built-in **safety checks and alerts**. If price feeds become unstable or an exchange API fails, the engine can pause minting/redemptions to avoid trouble[_[76][77]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,notify%20engineers%20of%20any%20issues). Engineers are alerted to intervene if something falls out of tolerance. In essence, the off-chain engine is the brain of Ethena, ensuring that for every USDe in circulation there is an appropriate short position and collateral backing it.

The interplay between **on-chain and off-chain** components is what allows Ethena to function seamlessly. When a user mints USDe, they interact with the on-chain contract (locking their collateral and getting tokens), but under the hood this triggers the off-chain engine to execute hedges on exchanges. Similarly, when redeeming, the on-chain burn triggers off-chain unwinding. This design means users experience USDe as a normal ERC-20 token with decentralized custody (they hold their USDe in their wallet, and if they want their collateral back they call the contract to redeem), while Ethena as a system quietly does all the complex trading in the background. The **transparency** is maintained via on-chain data (anyone can see the total USDe supply and the composition of collateral held at custodians, which Ethena publishes via proofs)[_[60]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=The%20%E2%80%9Chedge%20fund%E2%80%9D%20is%20fully,transparent), and the **efficiency** is achieved by tapping into off-chain liquidity pools that dwarf on-chain markets (major CEX perps can handle billions in volume, enabling Ethena’s scale).

## Delta-Neutral Strategy and Yield Generation Mechanics

**Delta-Neutral Hedging:** The foundational principle of Ethena’s stability mechanism is delta-neutral hedging. As formally described in financial terms, Ethena strives to maintain Δ = Long Collateral — Short Perpetuals ≈ 0 at all times[_[78]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=In%20Ethena%20stablecoin%20delta,relies%20on%20maintaining%20the%20formula). Here, the _long collateral_ is the basket of crypto assets deposited by USDe minters (e.g. ETH, BTC, staked ETH, etc.), and the _short perpetuals_ are the aggregate short positions Ethena holds on futures markets for those same assets[_[78]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=In%20Ethena%20stablecoin%20delta,relies%20on%20maintaining%20the%20formula). When Δ = 0, the portfolio is indifferent to small movements in the underlying asset’s price — any change in the value of the collateral is offset by an opposite change in the value of the short. In practice, perfect hedges are hard to maintain continuously (due to fees, slippage, discrete rebalancing, etc.), but Ethena’s engine dynamically adjusts positions to keep the net exposure minimal[_[79]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,further%20in%20this%20article)[_[74]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,prevent%20minting%20or%20redeeming%20if). The robustness of this design was demonstrated in simple examples: if ETH’s price jumps +10%, the value of ETH collateral goes +10% but the short loses 10%, roughly canceling out; if price drops –10%, the collateral loses value but the short gains equivalently[_[23]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,%E2%86%92%20net%20value%20%E2%89%88%20%24100). This ensures **USDe remains fully backed** — the protocol should always hold assets roughly equal in value to the USDe outstanding, meaning users can redeem 1 USDe for $1 worth of collateral even after significant market moves. Of course, extreme events can challenge this: if an asset’s price moves so violently or gaps so suddenly that the short can’t be adjusted in time, the hedge could temporarily be off and the system under- or over-collateralized. Ethena mitigates this by over-collateralizing slightly and by having circuit breakers (pause minting if markets are too erratic)[_[80]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,notify%20engineers%20of%20any%20issues). Overall, delta-neutral hedging is a proven method used by quantitative funds, and Ethena has essentially ported it into a decentralized context.

**Yield Sources:** Maintaining a peg would be pointless for a synthetic stablecoin if it didn’t also _earn yield_, since competing with fiat-backed coins requires compensating users for the extra complexity/risk. Ethena’s protocol revenue comes from **three primary sources**, which map to the components of its portfolio[_[81]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=According%20to%20Ethena%E2%80%99s%20documentation%2C%20there,level%20yield)[_[82]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=The%20revenue%20generated%20by%20the,protocol%20originates%20from%20three%20sources):

1.   **Perpetual Futures Funding and Basis Spread:** This is by far the largest source of yield. By holding short perpetual positions, Ethena earns the periodic funding payments whenever the market is bullish (positive funding rate)[_[83]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Futures%20funding%20and%20basis%20spread,without%20taking%20directional%20market%20risk). In essence, traders who are _long_ pay Ethena (and other shorts) to keep the futures price tethered to spot. Additionally, if there is a fixed “basis spread” (futures trading above spot even without continuous funding), the protocol can monetize that by rolling futures positions. According to Ethena’s documentation, as of early 2025 roughly ~92% of backing capital was allocated to this derivatives-driven strategy[_[84]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=2,the%20delta%20hedging%20derivatives%20positions). The actual returns from funding can vary widely over time: in hot markets, funding rates can be high (e.g. in 2021, ETH perp funding averaged ~16% APY to shorts) whereas in bear markets they can flip negative or negligible (in 2022, during a downturn, the strategy yielded virtually 0%)[_[85]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=Historically%20due%20to%20the%20mismatch,are%20short%20this%20delta%20exposure). Notably, 2024 saw an average ~13% APY from funding on ETH perps as crypto markets recovered[_[85]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=Historically%20due%20to%20the%20mismatch,are%20short%20this%20delta%20exposure). This volatility in funding yields means Ethena’s payout to USDe stakers isn’t fixed; it floats with market conditions. However, because crypto markets often have strong leverage demand, especially in uptrends, the funding source has on average been quite lucrative — enough to give USDe an estimated ~10% APY since inception[_[4]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Among%20the%20new%20entrants%2C%20Ethena,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months), roughly double the yield of a treasury-backed stablecoin over the same period[_[4]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Among%20the%20new%20entrants%2C%20Ethena,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months).
2.   **Staking Rewards on Collateral:** Many assets deposited as collateral can earn native yields. For example, Ethena accepts **staked Ether (stETH)** and similar liquid staking tokens, which accrue Ethereum’s proof-of-stake rewards (~4–5% APY in mid-2020s). Even if the asset isn’t deposited in staked form, Ethena can choose to stake a portion of idle collateral (e.g. converting some ETH to stETH) to earn consensus layer rewards[_[86]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=1)[_[87]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=In%202021%20this%20income%20averaged,Ethereum%20has%20grown%20over%20time). These rewards are paid in the asset (ETH), which Ethena can periodically sell for more collateral or use to augment backing. However, this source is relatively small compared to funding. Ethena reported that only about 6% of USDe’s backing assets were in staked tokens as of Jan 2025[_[47]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=1,execution%20layer%20rewards). This contributed on the order of a few percent APY (e.g. if stETH yields 5% on 6% of assets, that’s ~0.3% toward overall USDe yield). Still, every bit helps, and it’s essentially “risk-free” yield from the protocol’s perspective (aside from smart contract risk of staking protocols).
3.   **Fixed Income on Liquid Reserves (“Liquid Stables”):** To ensure liquidity and handle situations when the basis trade yield is low or negative, Ethena keeps a portion of reserves in **stablecoins or tokenized treasuries** that earn fixed interest[_[48]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=3,Stables). For instance, Ethena launched **USDtb**, a stablecoin fully backed by BlackRock’s tokenized money market fund (BIDL)[_[88]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Ethena%20is%20a%20decentralized%20protocol%2C,the%20largest%20synthetic%20dollar%20USDe)[_[48]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=3,Stables). USDtb is effectively a tokenized T-bill fund, yielding around the Fed interest rate (~5%). By allocating some capital to USDtb or holding some USDC on deposit (Coinbase offers institutional holders a yield through its USDC loyalty program), Ethena can earn a modest fixed return that bolsters the stablecoin’s peg stability[_[48]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=3,Stables). According to Ethena, about 7% of the backing was in such liquid stable assets by late 2024[_[89][48]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=3,Stables). This also provides a cushion: if crypto markets enter a prolonged period of negative funding (e.g. if traders are predominantly short, meaning Ethena would _have to pay_ funding as a short holder), the protocol could lean more on RWA yields like USDtb to continue providing some baseline return and possibly reduce short exposure for a time. Essentially, this is a diversification of revenue sources to avoid total reliance on one market condition.

The **combination** of these yield streams is what allows USDe to pay attractive interest to holders. The revenue is gathered by the protocol and periodically distributed to sUSDe stakers via the **StakingRewardsDistributor** contract (users can claim or it auto-compounds)[_[90]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=All%20rewards%20are%20distributed%20via,chain%20at%20any%20time). All distribution is transparent on-chain, meaning anyone can verify the accrued rewards. In the last year, Ethena generated roughly \$450 million in revenue[_[91]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=twice%20that%20of%20treasury,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months) — a figure comparable to or exceeding the revenues of many decentralized exchanges or lending protocols — with all of it (so far) going to users or into reserves, since governance hasn’t yet turned on any protocol fee capture.

From a financial perspective, **USDe’s yield represents a transfer of value from leveraged traders (and other yield payers) to stablecoin holders**. This concept aligns with broader observations in crypto markets: stablecoin holders implicitly take on counterparty and peg risks, and _should_ demand compensation for it[_[32]_](https://sharonyross.com/GKRRV_LeverageAndStablecoinPegs.pdf#:~:text=and%20money%20market%20funds,compensated%20for%20holding%20risky%20stablecoins)[_[92]_](https://sharonyross.com/GKRRV_LeverageAndStablecoinPegs.pdf#:~:text=We%20posit%20that%20a%20novel,stablecoins%20constitute%20the%20majority%20of). In fact, an academic study by Ross et al. (2023) found that stablecoin lending rates on exchanges (which reflect traders borrowing stablecoins to leverage) have been significantly positive and correlate strongly with perpetual futures funding rates[_[93]_](https://sharonyross.com/GKRRV_LeverageAndStablecoinPegs.pdf#:~:text=Figure%204%20shows%20a%20binscatter,formally%2C%20we%20test%20the%20model%E2%80%99s)[_[94]_](https://sharonyross.com/GKRRV_LeverageAndStablecoinPegs.pdf#:~:text=USDT%20Lending%20Ratet%20%3D%20%CE%B1,associated%20with%20an%20increase%20in). This means that in the open market, stablecoin holders _do_ earn interest (e.g. by lending USDT on an exchange margin market) as a function of speculative demand. Ethena essentially captures that relationship within the stablecoin itself: rather than requiring users to manually lend their stablecoins, USDe’s design programmatically earns those yields and funnels them to holders. In effect, **Ethena internalizes the crypto funding rate market into its stablecoin protocol**. This is a powerful idea because it turns what was previously an opportunity mainly for active traders or arbitrage desks into a **passive yield accessible to anyone holding USDe**. It democratizes a form of return that was otherwise fragmented across CeFi platforms.

## Example Walk-Through: Minting and Redeeming USDe

To illustrate how these components work together, let’s walk through a simplified user interaction:

*   **Minting USDe:** Suppose Alice wants $10,000 of stablecoins but also to earn yield on them. She deposits \$10,000 worth of ETH into Ethena’s contract. The on-chain contract verifies the current price of ETH via its oracle network (e.g. 1 ETH = \$2,000, so Alice deposits 5 ETH)[_[63]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=%2A%20On,of%20CeFi%20and%20DeFi%20markets). It mints 10,000 USDe to Alice’s address. Immediately, the off-chain engine sees the Mint event and executes roughly \$10,000 of ETH short positions on perhaps two exchanges, say \$5k on Binance and \$5k on Bybit, using Alice’s 5 ETH (held via custodian) as collateral for those shorts. Now Ethena is delta-neutral on Alice’s deposit. As time passes, Alice holds 10k USDe in her wallet, which she can use like any stablecoin (trade, send, etc.). If she stakes it in the sUSDe contract, she will start accruing yield. Meanwhile, Ethena’s short positions collect funding payments every hour; those go into the custodian, increasing the collateral. Let’s say after a month, the funding rate was such that it earned 0.5% (so \$50 on her \$10k). The protocol would credit that to the sUSDe pool, increasing the amount of USDe Alice can redeem when she unstakes.
*   **Redeeming USDe:** Now suppose Bob wants to redeem 20,000 USDe for its underlying collateral (perhaps he arbitraged a slight peg deviation or he simply wants to exit to ETH). Bob sends 20,000 USDe to the Ethena contract’s redeem function. The contract checks the price and calculates how much ETH is owed — say ETH = \$2,500 at this later time, so 20k USDe corresponds to 8 ETH. The contract burns Bob’s USDe and emits a Redeem event for 8 ETH. The off-chain engine picks this up and now _buys back_ \$20k worth of ETH perps on exchanges to close out the hedge (freeing 8 ETH of collateral). The 8 ETH is released by the custodian to Bob’s wallet (possibly via an intermediate vault contract). Bob receives his collateral, and the USDe supply decreases by 20k. Notice that even though ETH’s price moved from 2k to 2.5k (25% up), Bob still got exactly \$20k worth of ETH (8 ETH) because Ethena’s collateral pool increased on net by that much during the price move (the short positions lost money, but the ETH collateral gained value; the system likely had more than 8 ETH backing 20k USDe before redemption due to earnings, etc., but ensures at least 1:1 value at redemption time).

This example glosses over some complexities (fees, slippage, etc.), but it highlights that **Ethena’s system continuously balances the books so that every USDe is always backed by ~$1 of net assets**. The combination of smart contracts (ensuring proper accounting and token logic) with an off-chain trading engine (ensuring market exposure is hedged) is what makes this possible.

## Risk Management and Stability Considerations

Any system that promises both stability and yield is inherently taking on and managing risk — otherwise the yield would not exist (risk-free arbitrage opportunities do not last indefinitely). Ethena’s technical stack reflects a deep acknowledgment of these risks, and the design includes multiple layers of protection. Nonetheless, understanding the residual risks is critical for evaluating the robustness of synthetic dollars.

**Market and Liquidity Risk:** Ethena’s primary revenue comes from the **crypto derivatives markets**, which are volatile and can experience liquidity crunches. If a sudden market crash occurs (as on Oct 10, 2025 when ~$20B of crypto positions were liquidated in hours[_[95]_](https://info.arkm.com/research/how-stablecoins-reached-a-300-billion-market-cap-in-2025#:~:text=notable%20has%20been%20Ethena%27s%20synthetic,of%20the%20market)[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions)), funding rates can swing wildly or even invert (shorts paying longs). Ethena could face short-term losses if, for example, funding turns negative while its positions are still open. Moreover, extreme price moves can outpace the hedging engine — e.g., if an asset price gaps down 50% overnight, the collateral loses 50% while the short can only profit 50% (since one cannot gain more than the notional shorted). Ethena might then be temporarily under-collateralized until it adjusts. The protocol’s response is to maintain some **safety margins** (excess collateral and the ability to pause operations). Indeed, during the **October 11, 2025 crash**, USDe briefly traded below \$1 on certain exchanges (reportedly as low as \$0.65 in isolated cases) due to a wave of redemptions and an oracle lag[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions). However, on more liquid venues like Curve (a DEX) the price stayed near \$1, and Ethena processed large redemptions smoothly with on-chain redemptions honoring the peg[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions). This suggests the peg deviation was a localized issue (likely an off-chain price mismatch or slippage on Binance’s trading pair) rather than a fundamental collapse of backing. Still, it underscores that **market volatility is a major risk** — the peg is only as good as the system’s ability to trade through turmoil. Ethena’s multi-exchange hedging and real-time monitoring are meant to mitigate this, and in practice the system proved resilient: despite the largest one-day deleveraging in crypto history, USDe’s design held up without insolvency[_[96]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Ethena%20stands%20as%20the%20largest,overall%2C%20after%20USDC%20and%20USDT)[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions).

**Counterparty and Custodial Risk:** By operating on centralized exchanges and using custodians, Ethena introduces points of trust and potential failure outside the blockchain. If an exchange freezes withdrawals or suffers a hack, Ethena could lose the funds or at least lose the ability to continue hedging there. This is not a hypothetical concern — in February 2025, Bybit (one of Ethena’s key partner exchanges) was hacked for ~$1.4 billion from its hot wallets[_[27]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Daily%2C%20profits%20and%20losses%20,assets%20remain%20with%20the%20custodian). This tested Ethena’s “exchange default” risk model in a real scenario. Fortunately, because Ethena’s collateral was held off-exchange via the custodian, **it did not lose funds in the Bybit hack**[_[27]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Daily%2C%20profits%20and%20losses%20,assets%20remain%20with%20the%20custodian). The protocol temporarily paused operations with Bybit, re-routed new hedges to other venues, and continued honoring mint/redemptions (albeit with possibly some delays). This outcome validated the decision to not keep collateral directly on exchanges. However, had the custodian itself been compromised or if multiple exchanges failed simultaneously, the situation could be more severe. Ethena mitigates this by using reputable custodians and spreading exposure, but some trust in off-chain entities is unavoidable. In essence, Ethena trades **decentralization for efficiency** to an extent — unlike a purely on-chain stablecoin (DAI), it _needs_ CeFi venues to generate yield. This introduces a form of **CeFi risk** (centralized finance risk) into a DeFi protocol. The hope is that with proper safeguards and perhaps insurance or backstops, this risk is manageable. Notably, Ethena’s documentation explicitly cites _“reliance on centralized exchanges and custodians”_ as a top risk category[_[97]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=No%20stablecoin%20model%20is%20flawless%2C,the%20main%20risk%20categories%20are). Users must understand that holding USDe is indirectly exposing them to the reliability of the exchanges it trades on. If a major exchange like Binance were to suddenly shut down, USDe might need to pause operations and could experience turbulence until hedges are re-established.

**Smart Contract and Technical Risk:** As with any DeFi protocol, bugs or exploits in Ethena’s smart contracts could be catastrophic. Ethena has undergone audits and has a bug bounty, but the complexity of its system (with cross-chain or cross-system interactions) means there are more potential attack surfaces. One scenario to consider is an **oracle manipulation attack**: if an attacker managed to feed incorrect price data that fooled Ethena’s collateral calculations, they might mint USDe with under-valued collateral or vice versa. Ethena’s multi-oracle design reduces this risk by requiring consensus across sources[_[63]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=%2A%20On,of%20CeFi%20and%20DeFi%20markets)[_[66]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Before%20any%20mint%20or%20redeem,prices%20differ%2C%20the%20operation%20stops), which is much safer than relying on a single price feed. Another risk is a **governance attack** on ENA (if governance can change critical parameters, an attacker acquiring a majority of ENA could potentially mismanage the system). Ethena’s team and community have thus far kept tight control to prevent hostile governance moves, and they’ve staged the fee switch and other changes based on pre-set milestones to ensure alignment[_[98]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=twice%20that%20of%20treasury,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months).

**“Dual Mandate” Tension — Stability vs. Yield:** A core insight from researchers is that stablecoins which chase yield inherently take on more risk, which can undermine stability[_[5]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=Insight%203%3A%20The%20integration%20of,fundamentally%20redefines%20the%20asset%E2%80%99s%20evolutionary). Ethena’s performance during benign periods has been excellent: it delivered ~10% APY to users and maintained its peg, validating the model[_[4]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Among%20the%20new%20entrants%2C%20Ethena,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months). But during the **worst-case stress test**, some cracks showed (the peg on one exchange broke, even though overall it recovered). The fundamental question is whether a stablecoin that **actively engages in market risk-taking** can be as robust as one that simply sits on cash. Ethena’s proponents would argue that the protocol’s risk management — exemplified by surviving events like the Bybit hack and Oct 10 deleveraging without loss of user funds — proves that with proper design, a yield-bearing stablecoin can be nearly as safe as a fiat-backed one[_[27]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Daily%2C%20profits%20and%20losses%20,assets%20remain%20with%20the%20custodian)[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions). Critics, however, caution that **complexity can breed unexpected risks** and point to Terra’s collapse as a warning. Ethena is different from Terra in critical ways (fully collateralized vs. under-collateralized, delta-hedged vs. speculative, etc.), but it’s not immune to failure. For instance, a prolonged bear market with persistently negative funding rates could make it hard to offer any yield, possibly causing users to abandon USDe for simpler alternatives. Ethena has a built-in response: shifting more collateral into USDtb or similar stable yield sources during such times[_[48]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=3,Stables). This would effectively transform USDe into more of a regular fiat-backed stablecoin temporarily (with lower yield), until market conditions improve. Such adaptability is promising.

**Regulatory Risk:** Though not a technical risk per se, regulation looms over stablecoins. Governments may impose reserve requirements, ban certain structures, or demand issuers register and hold certain assets. Ethena’s model, straddling CeFi and DeFi, might attract scrutiny: it uses offshore exchanges, custodians, and creates a large synthetic dollar supply that isn’t directly redeemable for actual USD (only for crypto). However, one could argue Ethena is _less_ concerning to regulators than an under-reserved fiat coin, because each USDe is fully backed by crypto assets verifiable on-chain[_[60]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=The%20%E2%80%9Chedge%20fund%E2%80%9D%20is%20fully,transparent). The U.S. GENIUS Act (if enacted) or similar stablecoin legislation might require disclosures and could classify USDe in certain ways. For now, synthetic stablecoins operate in a gray zone — they’re not issued by banks, but they also don’t hold traditional securities (so they might avoid certain regulations that hit fiat-backed coins). Regulatory clarity will be a factor in the long-term sustainability of all stablecoins, including synthetic ones.

In summary, Ethena’s technical stack incorporates **robust risk management principles**: distributed exchange risk, continuous monitoring, dynamic rebalancing, and transparency. The fact that Ethena navigated real-life crises in 2025 without collapsing has bolstered confidence in its model[_[27]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Daily%2C%20profits%20and%20losses%20,assets%20remain%20with%20the%20custodian)[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions). Multicoin Capital’s analysis noted that each major stress-test survived adds to Ethena’s credibility, brand capital, and “anti-fragility” — much as big banks prove their resilience through stress tests[_[99]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Labs%20%E2%80%94%20has%2C%20in%20our,overall%2C%20after%20USDC%20and%20USDT)[_[100]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=collateral%20on%20major%20exchanges,driver%20of%20its%20rapid%20growth). Indeed, Ethena’s successful handling of those events has likely contributed to its rapid adoption as a collateral asset on exchanges and DeFi platforms, as partners see it as battle-tested. Still, caution is warranted: **no stablecoin model is flawless**[_[101]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Risks%2C%20Constraints%2C%20and%20Design%20Trade). Ethena must continue evolving its risk frameworks, possibly adding insurance funds or circuit breakers for worst-case scenarios (e.g. auto-pausing when funding flips to a certain negative threshold, to avoid bleeding reserves). The project’s Risk Committee and community governance play a key role in this ongoing risk management.

## Economic and Technological Implications

The emergence of synthetic dollar stablecoins like USDe carries significant implications for both the crypto ecosystem and traditional finance interfaces. Here we discuss a few notable ones:
