**Competition in the Stablecoin Market:** Synthetic dollars directly challenge the stablecoin incumbents by attacking their weakness — the lack of yield sharing. As noted earlier, crypto users have a high opportunity cost of capital[_[102]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=through%20higher%20yields,higher%20than%20US%20Treasury%20yields). If a stablecoin only yields 0–5% while DeFi or other crypto opportunities yield more, users gravitate to the higher yields (adjusted for risk). Ethena offered a substantially higher yield (~10%) than treasuries in 2024–25[_[4]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Among%20the%20new%20entrants%2C%20Ethena,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months), making it attractive to yield-seeking users. This fueled its growth in circulation and liquidity, to the point where USDe became a top-3 stablecoin by market cap[_[9]_](https://info.arkm.com/research/how-stablecoins-reached-a-300-billion-market-cap-in-2025#:~:text=The%20big%20change%20this%20year,of%20the%20market). This growth validates J.P. Morgan’s projection that yield-bearing stablecoins could capture a large share of the market[_[28]_](https://k33.com/research/articles/cap-a-new-stablecoin-frontier#:~:text=%2A%20Yield,ecosystem%3A%20minters%2C%20operators%2C%20and%20restakers)[_[29]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=funding%20rates%20app,rates). In response, we may see the traditional issuers adjust — perhaps Circle or Tether will explore sharing some yield or launching separate yield-bearing products to stay competitive. Already, we observe new entrants following Ethena’s lead: e.g. **CAP’s cUSD** (mentioned by K33 Research) is another yield-bearing stablecoin focusing on decentralization and using USDT/USDC as minting inputs[_[103]_](https://k33.com/research/articles/cap-a-new-stablecoin-frontier#:~:text=JPMorgan%20projects%20that%20yield,on%20decentralization%20for%20retail%E2%80%99s%20benefit)[_[28]_](https://k33.com/research/articles/cap-a-new-stablecoin-frontier#:~:text=%2A%20Yield,ecosystem%3A%20minters%2C%20operators%2C%20and%20restakers). The landscape in a few years could be a split between _low-yield, low-risk_ stablecoins and _high-yield, crypto-native_ stablecoins. If JPMorgan’s 50% market share prediction holds, trillions of dollars could end up in these yield-generating stablecoins[_[29]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=funding%20rates%20app,rates). This raises interesting questions: will users treat these like savings accounts (hold long-term for yield) or primarily as trading collateral? How will increased competition affect funding rates themselves (if many stablecoins start harvesting funding, the available yield might be shared/thinned out)? These economic dynamics will evolve as more players enter. For now, Ethena’s USDe has carved out a clear niche and first-mover advantage in the “yield-bearing, delta-neutral” category.

**Integration with Exchanges and CeFi:** One measure of success for a stablecoin is whether it’s accepted as **collateral on major exchanges**[_[104]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=In%20our%20view%2C%20the%20true,driver%20of%20its%20rapid%20growth). In that regard, USDe made remarkable inroads: By 2024, **Binance** and **Bybit** both added USDe as a collateral option for margin and futures trading, and even introduced USDe trading pairs[_[105]_](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2024/05/07/ethenas-ena-jumps-8-as-bybit-endorses-usde-token-as-collateral-for-derivatives-trading#:~:text=,Joshua%20Lim%20of%20Arbelos%20said)[_[106]_](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2024/05/07/ethenas-ena-jumps-8-as-bybit-endorses-usde-token-as-collateral-for-derivatives-trading#:~:text=token%27s%20utility). This is significant because it closes a loop: USDe is both created through trading markets and then _used_ in those same markets as a base currency. Bybit’s team, for example, saw value in letting traders post USDe as collateral, since USDe inherently carries an embedded short position that could reduce tail risks for the trader’s own positions[_[107]_](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2024/05/07/ethenas-ena-jumps-8-as-bybit-endorses-usde-token-as-collateral-for-derivatives-trading#:~:text=Bybit%27s%20move%20to%20accept%20USDe,in%20an%20interview%20with%20CoinDesk). In other words, a trader using USDe as collateral is somewhat hedged against market drops (because if crypto prices crash, USDe’s backing short gains, theoretically keeping the collateral value more intact)[_[108]_](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2024/05/07/ethenas-ena-jumps-8-as-bybit-endorses-usde-token-as-collateral-for-derivatives-trading#:~:text=Lim%20pointed%20out%20that%20the,embedded%20short%20position%20in%20itself). This is a fascinating synergy: a stablecoin that is _designed for traders_, being adopted by exchanges to enhance trading. Ethena’s partnerships with exchanges also extend to strategic investments — several exchanges have invested in ENA tokens, aligning their incentives[_[99]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Labs%20%E2%80%94%20has%2C%20in%20our,overall%2C%20after%20USDC%20and%20USDT). The integration into CeFi platforms signals a blurring of lines between CeFi and DeFi. Ethena’s success can be seen as **CeDeFi** in action: a decentralized stablecoin protocol working hand-in-hand with centralized exchanges. This model could be a template for future projects where DeFi protocols leverage CeFi for what it’s best at (liquidity, user access) while CeFi platforms leverage DeFi innovations to offer new products. However, it also means Ethena is somewhat dependent on the goodwill and stability of these exchanges — a symbiosis that must be carefully managed.

**“Perpification” and Scaling of the Basis Trade:** A potential constraint on Ethena’s growth is the size of the crypto perpetual futures market. There is only so much open interest (OI) available to take the other side of Ethena’s shorts. By late 2025, crypto perps OI across major venues was on the order of \$100 billion[_[109]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Perpetual%20futures%20have%20achieved%20strong,what%20we%20mean%20by%20perpification), and daily volumes often exceed \$100B[_[109]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Perpetual%20futures%20have%20achieved%20strong,what%20we%20mean%20by%20perpification). Ethena’s share of that (with \$8–15B hedge positions) is significant but not yet straining the system. If USDe were to 10x in supply, could the futures markets absorb \$100B of new shorts? Possibly, but funding rates would adjust (likely becoming less favorable as the short side gets crowded). Ethena acknowledges this short-term TAM issue[_[110]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=A%20common%20question%20about%20Ethena,term). However, they and others foresee **perpetual futures markets expanding to new asset classes**, a trend dubbed “_perpification_”[_[10]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Perpetuals%20on%20Tokenized%20Equities). For example, if tokenized stocks become mainstream, one could have perpetual swaps on Apple or S&P 500 indices traded on crypto rails[_[10]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Perpetuals%20on%20Tokenized%20Equities). The global equity market (~\$100 trillion) is vastly larger than crypto, and demand for leveraged equity exposure is huge (as evidenced by the popularity of 0DTE options in stock markets)[_[111]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Perpetuals%20on%20Tokenized%20Equities). If even a fraction of equities trading moves to on-chain perpetuals, the capacity for basis trades grows by orders of magnitude[_[112]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=As%20equities%20become%20tokenized%2C%20equity,trade%20by%20several%20orders%20of). Ethena’s platform could theoretically plug into those markets as well, providing liquidity and hedging for tokenized equity perps or commodity perps. In essence, Ethena could become a **universal synthetic dollar provider** across asset classes. This would not only grow USDe’s supply but diversify its revenue (earning funding from non-crypto markets too). There are significant hurdles (legal, liquidity, infrastructure) before that vision materializes, but the trajectory is visible: more assets being tokenized (BlackRock’s CEO Larry Fink has called tokenization the “next generation for markets”[_[43]_](https://decrypt.co/116145/blackrock-ceo-says-next-generationmarkets-is-tokenization#:~:text=Discover%20SCENE)), and more derivatives moving on-chain or into hybrid models. Ethena is positioning itself at the nexus of these trends — effectively as a _middleware_ that connects tokenized asset exposure with a stablecoin interface.

**Fintech Integration and Mass Adoption:** Beyond crypto-native venues, yield-bearing stablecoins might attract interest from fintech companies and emerging markets. Imagine a payment app or neobank offering accounts denominated in a stablecoin that pays interest. In high-inflation countries, a dollar stablecoin that yields 10% could be extremely attractive as a savings vehicle[_[113]_](https://research.grayscale.com/reports/holding-steady-the-rise-of-stablecoins#:~:text=But%20stablecoins%20have%20potential%20to,to%20local%20currencies%2C%20stablecoins%20may)[_[15]_](https://www.reuters.com/technology/stablecoin-tether-gets-boost-dollar-alternative-emerging-markets-ceo-says-2024-04-19/#:~:text=,life%20in%20the%20desert%20city). Fintech apps could integrate Ethena’s stablecoin via APIs or even natively if regulations allow. There is precedent: some wallets (e.g. Phantom wallet) have integrated decentralized exchanges for trading directly[_[114]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=directly). If Phantom or another wallet integrated USDe for savings or collateral, it could bring more retail users to Ethena indirectly. Additionally, **DeFi composability** means USDe can be used in lending protocols, yield farms, etc., potentially amplifying its utility (though ironically, USDe already generates yield, so secondary lending might be less needed except for leverage). Ethena launched a **Whitelabel Stablecoin service** — effectively Stablecoin-as-a-Service — where they help other projects or blockchains issue their own yield-bearing stablecoins powered by Ethena’s infrastructure[_[99]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Labs%20%E2%80%94%20has%2C%20in%20our,overall%2C%20after%20USDC%20and%20USDT). For example, Sui’s foundation issued “SUIUSD” (via a partner, StablecoinX) which is reportedly backed by Ethena’s reserves (SUI gets converted to USDe under the hood)[_[99]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Labs%20%E2%80%94%20has%2C%20in%20our,overall%2C%20after%20USDC%20and%20USDT). This indicates Ethena is modularizing its stack for others to use, which could accelerate adoption.

**Value Capture and Decentralization:** A question arises on how decentralized and sustainable synthetic stablecoins are. Ethena Labs is a centralized team that bootstrapped USDe, though the protocol has a DAO structure in principle. Over time, more control could migrate to ENA token holders, and the system could operate autonomously with open-source algorithms for hedging. However, the heavy off-chain component means some centralization will likely remain (at least in oracles and custodian relationships). The **value capture** is also a point of discussion: currently USDe holders get all the yield, and ENA token (governance) accrues speculative value on future fee capture. Once the “fee switch” is flipped, ENA holders might take a cut of revenues[_[98]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=twice%20that%20of%20treasury,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months). This would effectively make ENA like a stock in the protocol’s performance. If Ethena continues to be one of the highest revenue generators in DeFi (it already rivaled or exceeded MakerDAO’s and Uniswap’s revenues in 2024–25[_[98]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=twice%20that%20of%20treasury,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months)), then ENA could be quite valuable, aligning governance incentives to keep USDe growing. However, introducing a fee will lower USDe’s yield unless offset by other improvements, so the trade-off between rewarding token holders and keeping the stablecoin competitive will need careful calibration.

In a broader sense, synthetic dollars like USDe blur the line between **stablecoins and investment funds**. They ask us to reconsider what a “stable” asset is — is it just about price stability, or also about maintaining purchasing power via yield? In high inflation environments, a stablecoin that yields could indeed better preserve real value than one that doesn’t (e.g. 1 USDC is 1 USD always, but if USD inflation is 5%, you lost 5% real value in a year; whereas 1 USDe might become 1.10 USDe in a year if yield is auto-compounded, roughly keeping pace with inflation). This arguably makes synthetic stablecoins attractive as a form of **digital dollar savings**. On the other hand, added complexity might make them less suitable for day-to-day payments or for more risk-averse users. It’s plausible we will see a stratification: some users stick to simple fiat-backed coins for transactions and stability, while others opt into higher-yield stablecoins for holding funds longer-term — much like one might keep some cash in a checking account and put the rest in a money market fund.

## Conclusion

Synthetic dollar stablecoins represent a bold innovation at the intersection of decentralized finance and traditional trading. By analyzing Ethena’s USDe, we have uncovered how a carefully constructed technical stack can transform a classic financial strategy — the delta-neutral basis trade — into a blockchain-based stablecoin that offers both stability and yield. The architecture comprises **Ethereum smart contracts** for transparent issuance and staking, a sophisticated **off-chain hedging engine** that interacts with global crypto markets in real time, and a layered **oracle/custody system** that bridges trust between on-chain and off-chain worlds[_[51]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Ethena%20combines%20on,liquidity%20and%20delta%20neutral%20hedging)[_[69]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Ethena%20uses%20third,full%20control%20of%20the%20collateral). This fusion enables USDe to hold its $1 peg through continuous rebalancing, while earning substantial revenues from funding rates, staking yields, and other exogenous sources[_[44]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,it%20further%20in%20this%20article)[_[85]_](https://docs.ethena.fi/solution-overview/protocol-revenue-explanation#:~:text=Historically%20due%20to%20the%20mismatch,are%20short%20this%20delta%20exposure).

The technical and financial rigor of Ethena’s design has been validated in practice: USDe rapidly gained adoption, being embraced by major exchanges and reaching tens of billions in circulation[_[9]_](https://info.arkm.com/research/how-stablecoins-reached-a-300-billion-market-cap-in-2025#:~:text=The%20big%20change%20this%20year,of%20the%20market)[_[105]_](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2024/05/07/ethenas-ena-jumps-8-as-bybit-endorses-usde-token-as-collateral-for-derivatives-trading#:~:text=,Joshua%20Lim%20of%20Arbelos%20said), and it withstood extreme market stress without failure (albeit with momentary dislocations)[_[8]_](https://phemex.com/news/article/usde-stablecoin-market-cap-plummets-45-to-below-9-billion-33553#:~:text=The%20market%20capitalization%20of%20USDe%2C,stablecoins%20in%20volatile%20market%20conditions). These outcomes suggest that yield-bearing stablecoins can indeed achieve a level of resilience approaching that of more static designs, provided they are engineered with robust risk management (e.g. diversified hedges, conservative pauses, and transparent collateralization)[_[71]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=Ethena%E2%80%99s%20exposure%20is%20roughly%20one,protections%20to%20keep%20USDe%20hedged)[_[80]_](https://rocknblock.io/blog/stablecoin-architecture-how-ethena-usde-works#:~:text=,notify%20engineers%20of%20any%20issues). Ethena’s success is paving the way for a broader shift in the stablecoin sector, one where sharing yield with users may become not just a niche offering but an industry norm[_[29]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=funding%20rates%20app,rates).

However, as our analysis shows, this model is not without its **trade-offs and challenges**. Relying on perpetual futures and centralized venues means synthetic stablecoins inherit risks from those domains — market squeezes, exchange defaults, regulatory changes — which must be meticulously managed. The complexity of the system introduces potential points of failure that simpler stablecoins avoid. Moreover, the pursuit of yield inherently creates a tension with the mandate of stability[_[5]_](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.17622v1#:~:text=Insight%203%3A%20The%20integration%20of,fundamentally%20redefines%20the%20asset%E2%80%99s%20evolutionary): in quiescent times both goals can be met, but in crises one may come at the expense of the other. So far, Ethena has managed to navigate this dual mandate admirably, but long-term success will require ongoing vigilance, adaptive governance, and possibly new innovations (such as insurance funds, decentralized perpetual markets, or further automation of risk responses).

From an academic and practical standpoint, the advent of synthetic stablecoins like USDe highlights the power of **tokenization of strategies**. Much like ETFs revolutionized access to complex portfolios in traditional finance, tokenized stablecoins with embedded strategies could revolutionize access to yield-bearing dollar assets in crypto[_[43]_](https://decrypt.co/116145/blackrock-ceo-says-next-generationmarkets-is-tokenization#:~:text=Discover%20SCENE)[_[2]_](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2024/05/07/ethenas-ena-jumps-8-as-bybit-endorses-usde-token-as-collateral-for-derivatives-trading#:~:text=The%20protocol%27s%20USDe%20token%2C%20often,funding%20rates%20for%20a%20yield). This could deepen the integration between crypto markets and traditional markets — for instance, a tokenized treasury strategy or a tokenized stock index perp could be wrapped into a stablecoin-like token, broadening the use of crypto rails for diverse financial products. Ethena’s model also underscores the importance of **interdisciplinary thinking** in fintech: achieving its goals required expertise in software (smart contracts, oracle design), finance (derivatives trading, risk hedging), and economics (incentive design, market structure). It serves as a case study in how blending these disciplines can create something novel that neither pure traditional finance nor pure on-chain finance could achieve alone.

In conclusion, the technical stack of synthetic dollar stablecoins is a **marvel of modern decentralized finance engineering**. Ethena’s USDe demonstrates that it is possible to build a stablecoin that is not only _stable_ but also _productive_, turning idle capital into yield-generating collateral without sacrificing the peg. If the current trajectory continues, synthetic dollars could become a significant “third pillar” of the stablecoin market alongside fiat-backed and crypto-collateralized coins, effectively **challenging the stablecoin duopoly** of USDT and USDC with a value proposition of “your dollars, but earning yield”[_[31]_](https://multicoin.capital/2025/11/13/ethena-synthetic-dollars-challenge-stablecoins-duopoly/#:~:text=Among%20the%20new%20entrants%2C%20Ethena,just%20the%20past%20twelve%20months)[_[28]_](https://k33.com/research/articles/cap-a-new-stablecoin-frontier#:~:text=%2A%20Yield,ecosystem%3A%20minters%2C%20operators%2C%20and%20restakers). The road ahead will surely involve further tests — of liquidity, of regulatory acceptance, of competitive response — but the foundation laid by Ethena’s technical architecture provides a strong starting point. By carefully analyzing and learning from this architecture, developers and researchers can refine these systems, address their vulnerabilities, and perhaps extend the approach to new frontiers (from tokenized equities to central bank digital currencies interfaces). Ultimately, the rise of synthetic dollar stablecoins underscores a broader trend: the unification of global markets through crypto technology, where any asset or strategy can be _wrapped in code_ and made accessible to a worldwide user base. In this new paradigm, the humble “stablecoin” is evolving into a more powerful instrument — one that not only **stores value** reliably, but also **creates value** for its holders through innovative financial engineering.

all_results_deduped.title: The Protocol Economy: Hashed 2026
all_results_deduped.content:
StablecoinX was the exception. It represented a differentiated thesis: listed-equity access to alternative stablecoin economics via the Ethena ecosystem, with governance via ENA accumulation and transparent, delta-neutral yield strategies. Led by a uniquely credible team across trading, compliance, and risk — Rob Hadick (Dragonfly), advisors from Multicoin, Castle Island, and FalconX — StablecoinX stood apart as an institutional-grade expression of a compelling economic flywheel. Our DAT stance in 2025 reflected restraint, not rejection: we participate where the structure is sound, not where the narrative is loud.

## Memecoins — A Market Driven by Cultural Reflex, Not Fundamentals [...] This ratio may remain relevant in 2026 even as the market becomes more institutional and liquidity is distributed across more ecosystems. Rotations may be shorter or more selective, but the behavioral mechanics — flow concentration → liquidity breadth → narrative expansion — are unchanged. ETH/BTC still tends to indicate when capital is preparing to move down the risk curve. [...] As the global stablecoin float grows, a predictable portion of that liquidity may migrate into yield-bearing, securitized private credit, not because it’s “financial,” but because it is the first on-chain product with repeatable, risk-priced cash flows.

Tokenized private credit is not the end state of on-chain fixed income. It is simply the first scalable market, the category that proves on-chain capital formation can operate with institutional discipline. In 2026, this segment will become a cornerstone of the emerging protocol economy — linking stablecoin liquidity with real-world yield in a transparent, regulated, and composable structure.

## Foreword

When I look back on 2025, what stands out is not volatility but alignment. The industry moved past the noise and returned to fundamentals. Narratives rose and faded quickly, yet the same question kept resurfacing: _what is actually working?_

Amid this shift, one theme became unmistakably clear: _stablecoins_ are becoming the first real piece of digital asset infrastructure to reach escape velocity.

Across the U.S., Korea, Japan, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia, regulatory approaches differed, but their interest and support is the most positive of all time. Stablecoins are no longer an experiment in liquidity — they are emerging as a practical settlement layer for cross-border payments, corporate treasury, and consumer transactions. The idea of “digital money with programmable trust” is finally finding its way into real economic flows.

Two years ago, when we spoke about the sequence of “infrastructure → production → finance,” it felt conceptual. In 2025, that sequence became visible. Infrastructure matured, measurable use cases appeared, and financial activity began to form around real demand rather than reflexive speculation. Crypto, which once moved in reverse order, is slowly aligning with the logic of the real economy.

_AI_ adds a new dimension to this shift. If stablecoins provide the rails, AI determines who uses them and how.

Economic activity that once depended on human interaction is being reorganized around autonomous agents capable of transacting, coordinating, and executing with minimal friction. The basic unit of the digital economy is moving from “users” to “agents,” and stablecoins are becoming the default medium that enables them to operate.

This is the early shape of the _protocol economy_ — where digital money, on-chain infrastructure, and AI-driven autonomy converge into a single operating layer. Users may not notice the transition, but the underlying system is changing rapidly. We are moving toward an environment where settlement, payments, data management, and coordination occur on-chain by default, while the front-end experience remains familiar.

_The Protocol Economy: Hashed Thesis 2026_ is not an attempt to predict which narratives will dominate next year. It is a record of how we thought in 2025, the decisions we made, and the areas where we are choosing to place our conviction in 2026. What we’ve learned is that patience matters more than speed, and structure matters more than story.

In the year ahead, competition will shift — from ideas to _execution_, from momentum to _durability_, from vanity metrics to _real economic primitives_, and from human-driven systems to _human-and-AI systems operating together_. Hashed will position itself at that intersection — between stablecoin infrastructure, the emerging agent economy, and the broader convergence of real and digital markets — and prove our conviction through action.

_Simon Kim

CEO & Managing Partner_

## Part 1 — Yearly Recap: 2025, The Year the Market Got Real

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 5](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*T58cTfcONNaty3EhR0kwJw.png)

2025 marked the point where the market shifted decisively from a competition of ideas to a competition of execution. Fewer new projects launched, and many short-lived experiments faded — but the teams that remained proved their relevance through real users, real volume, and real revenue.

_Hyperliquid_ broke through the structural limits of on-chain derivatives by delivering CEX-level liquidity and matching performance. _Pump.fun_ turned meme-driven speculation into a repeatable high-traffic funnel with a working revenue model. _Axiom_ expanded automated trading strategies from professionals to everyday users, reshaping the very behavior of active market participants. The question driving the market changed: instead of “Is it novel?” the focus became _“Does it work — consistently?”_

This transition unfolded at the intersection of regulatory clarity and capital realignment. Major jurisdictions formalized standards for stablecoin issuance, audits, and reserve management. Institutional capital started to shift away from narrative-driven tokens toward practical solutions. While total investment volume declined, average round sizes and institutional participation increased, signaling a meaningful upgrade in the quality of capital. Market attention moved from scalable liquidity to _provable economics_.

Technological progress provided the foundation.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 6](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*enCfwlLEUQ8tNfyT0mqdSw.png)

Ethereum reduced costs and improved UX through zk-rollups, EIP-4844, and continued advancement in account abstraction. Solana showed that a monolithic chain can scale, sustaining high real-world throughput and surpassing 50,000+ TPS — making raw scalability less of a bottleneck. Bridging protocols like LayerZero and CCTP brought new expectations for settlement finality and interoperability across chains. The long-standing perception that crypto infrastructure was “slow and expensive” materially faded in 2025.

On this technical and regulatory foundation, stablecoins emerged as the functional settlement layer of the on-chain economy. Circulating supply has already surpassed roughly US$300 billion, and multiple sources report multi-billion-dollar daily flows across remittances, exchange settlement, corporate payments, and treasury operations. Yield-bearing stablecoins introduced a new paradigm — digital cash that earns. As a result, stablecoins evolved from a transactional intermediary into the starting point for capital allocation across markets.

Most importantly, 2025 was not a recovery phase; it was the first year of structural realignment. Inefficiencies and excessive speculations were flushed out. Market structure, technology, and capital flows synchronized around sustainable models. The on-chain economy moved from narrative-driven visions to operational systems — where traction, integration, and defensibility mattered more than storytelling.

This pattern defined 2025. Across stablecoin issuers, new L1 attempts, AI-crypto hybrids, DAT vehicles, and memecoin platforms, bursts of attention were common — but true coherent economics, durable user activity, and regulatory readiness were rare. Traction alone no longer implied staying power; the market began filtering projects by operational depth and long-term integration potential.

For Hashed, this shift sharpened our lens: our decisions to engage or abstain weren’t tactical reactions, but responses of a structural view of where real value could compound.

## Part 2 — Selective Conviction: Where We Drew the Line in 2025

2025 saw no shortage of narratives that captured attention. Stablecoins, new L1s, AI-crypto hybrids, DAT vehicles, and memecoins all had their moments in the spotlight. But only a small minority converted that momentum into real economics, regulatory credibility, or sustained user activity.

In a market where attention came easily but durability did not, choosing _when not to participate_ became as important as choosing where to commit. This chapter outlines the areas where we stepped back, the few cases where we leaned in, and the principles that guided those decisions as we look ahead to 2026.

## The Institutional Stablecoin Inflection

Stablecoins quietly evolved from a trading primitive into the most credible form of digital financial infrastructure. Circulating supply grew more than 60% YoY, and 12-month adjusted transaction volume approached US$9T — numbers that rival legacy settlement networks. For the first time, regulatory frameworks across major jurisdictions converged toward clarity: standardized rules for issuance, reserves, audits, and licensing replaced the ambiguity that defined the prior decade. Enterprises, no longer experimenting, began integrating programmable money into payments, lending, treasury, and consumer workflows.

Despite this momentum, Hashed remained disciplined and passed on most bank-backed or centralized stablecoin issuers. Many lacked regulatory depth, enterprise-grade infrastructure, or business model beyond issuance spreads. And while more than 90% of global corporates reported “interest” in stablecoins, only ~13% had actual usage — revealing the gap between intent and readiness. Our mandate — global-scale infrastructure, regulatory alignment, and defensible liquidity — required a higher bar.

Bastion met that bar. Rather than launching a standalone asset, Bastion built the rails: regulated issuance, custody, on/off-ramps, compliance primitives, and enterprise GTM — all under a New York trust structure with pending NYDFS approval. With adoption curves steepening, and with enterprises demanding white-label, programmable money solutions instead of speculative tokens, Bastion represented the structural moment we had been waiting for.

Stablecoins are no longer a product; they are becoming a new financial rail. Our participation reflects that belief.

## The New L1 Wave We Mostly Passed

In 2025, “L1 + X” emerged as a popular early-stage strategy. Many teams bundled new base layers with stablecoin ecosystems, exchange integrations, or RWA distribution in an attempt to accelerate liquidity and ecosystem formation from day one. With perp DEX volumes surging and institutional flows rising, the strategy made intuitive sense — but it also revealed a deeper trend.

Advances in cloud infrastructure, modular stacks, and validator networks have lowered the barrier to launching an L1. Early traction — wallet activations, subsidized transactions, ecosystem incentives — became easier to generate. What remained difficult was long-term retention, differentiation, and credible defensibility. Many new entrants converged toward similar architectures and incentive playbooks, creating activity without durable moats.

We passed on most of this wave for a simple reason: the real bottleneck for new L1s is no longer launch mechanics but sustained relevance. The market now rewards chains that can plug into real-world systems, data flows, and compliance environments — not just those that achieve high throughput or initial liquidity.

Rialo stood out because it expanded what a chain can connect to, not just how it performs. Its approach to treating external data and web calls as native operations opened a new design space that aligned with where we believe L1 differentiation will matter the most.

## AI x Crypto — Backing Real Infrastructure, Not Hype

2025 was the year AI x crypto became a magnet for capital — and for noise. Dozens of decentralized compute networks, AI-native L1s, and agent ecosystems launched, each promising an AI-aligned future. Few delivered measurable performance improvements over existing AI infra, and many lacked clarity on their economic edge, safety guarantees, or architectural necessity.

The one durable trend was the rise of AI agents — autonomous systems capable of transacting, negotiating, gathering data, and executing strategies. This shift exposed crypto’s missing piece: a programmable, sub-second payment layer enabling machine-to-machine coordination. Without identity, spending controls, and micro-settlement primitives, most “AI chains” and “agent ecosystems” remained conceptual.

We avoided momentum-driven AI projects and deepened our conviction in Kite AI, where we have been an investor since 2023. Rather than promising a new chain or compute network, Kite focused on the economic substrate AI agents actually require:

*   Cryptographic identities
*   Programmable spending policies
*   Stablecoin-denominated microtransactions
*   Settlement rails aligned with emerging standards like x402
*   Secure, auditable payment flows for autonomous systems

As AI adoption accelerates globally, we believe crypto’s highest-leverage role is not becoming “the AI chain” but powering the trust, identity, and transaction infrastructure that AI systems cannot build themselves. AI × crypto becomes real through payments first, compute later — and Kite is built for that first step.

## The DAT Boom We Chose to Watch, Not Chase

DATs (Digital Asset Treasuries) surged in 2025 as liquid market investors sought structured exposure to BTC, ETH, and SOL beta without the complexity of token custody. Inspired by Strategy’s playbook, more than a dozen public vehicles amassed over US$10B in NAV, leveraging corporate balance sheets to accumulate crypto assets and deploy them into staking and DeFi yields. Media narratives framed them as “leveraged crypto equities with professional management,” and investor inflows followed.

But beneath the excitement, structural weaknesses were clear:

*   Most DATs relied on simple accumulation strategies, exposing shareholders to amplified volatility.
*   Premiums to NAV expanded reflexively, echoing previous speculative cycles.
*   Regulatory boundaries around tokenized securities, investment companies, and public disclosures remained unsettled.
*   Many entities lacked risk frameworks suited for managing volatile, yield-bearing crypto balance sheets.

We passed on the majority of opportunities not because the idea lacked demand, but because most vehicles lacked durability, regulatory clarity, or operational depth.

StablecoinX was the exception. It represented a differentiated thesis: listed-equity access to alternative stablecoin economics via the Ethena ecosystem, with governance via ENA accumulation and transparent, delta-neutral yield strategies. Led by a uniquely credible team across trading, compliance, and risk — Rob Hadick (Dragonfly), advisors from Multicoin, Castle Island, and FalconX — StablecoinX stood apart as an institutional-grade expression of a compelling economic flywheel. Our DAT stance in 2025 reflected restraint, not rejection: we participate where the structure is sound, not where the narrative is loud.

## Memecoins — A Market Driven by Cultural Reflex, Not Fundamentals

Memecoins defined the retail landscape in 2024 and carried that momentum into 2025, but their market behavior diverged from earlier crypto cycles. These assets traded less on fundamentals and more on cultural reflex: sudden social moments that triggered fast ignition, concentrated liquidity, and rapid decay once attention shifted. Platforms like Pump.fun, Photon, and GMGN accelerated this dynamic by making issuance instant and limitless, turning memecoins into high-velocity cultural instruments rather than investable tokens.

By mid-2025, open issuance had begun to constrain the market more than it enabled it. Supply surged, attention half-lives shortened, and most assets reverted quickly as liquidity rotated to the next narrative. Only a small minority sustained relevance — and they did so not through token design, but through stronger cultural anchoring: recognizable identity, consistent storytelling, and communities that cohered beyond the initial spike. The pattern resembled the post-2022 PFP cycle, where almost everything collapsed except projects that built real distribution and brand affinity over time.

Our stance in 2025 reflected this reality. We declined most memecoin-related deals — not because upside disappeared, but because upside became increasingly non-investable: driven by spontaneous social dynamics rather than teams or product execution. Instead, our focus remained on founders who treated attention as a durable asset rather than a one-time catalyst, and who were building toward longer-lived cultural or distributional leverage rather than momentary speculation.

Memecoins reward creativity, but they expose structural weakness quickly. In 2025, our approach was shaped by that asymmetry.

## Part 3–2026 Structural Transition: The Year Applications Break Out

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 7](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*VltR8E0wSpziTyLrbqrMbA.png)

Crypto spent the past decade evolving in reverse. _Finance_ came first — reflexive liquidity loops, leverage-driven markets, and speculative collateral systems. _Production_ followed, from NFTs and gaming assets to early RWA experiments. _Infrastructure_ matured last, only recently reaching the point where scalability, interoperability, custody, and compliance are no longer limiting factors. But by late 2025, the order finally aligned with the logic of real economies: infrastructure enabled production, and production began generating the raw material from which real finance naturally emerges.

This is the first moment where the original sequence becomes visible: _infrastructure → production → finance_. The rails are now functional enough for real activity to accumulate, and that activity is finally rich enough for financial primitives — money markets, tokenized debt & credit, prediction markets, and on-chain settlement workflows — to take shape not as speculative abstractions, but as downstream expressions of actual demand.

Stablecoins provide the first universal liquidity pool; AI agents act as real-time demand processors; RWAs offer the earliest bridge to off-chain economic flows; enterprise integrations distribute these capabilities at scale. What emerges is not a new version of DeFi, but an economic system where applications — not chains, not tokens — begin to define the operating frontier of crypto.

2026 will bethe year this conversion becomes structural. The individual perspectives that follow reflect how each individual is positioning around this shift, and how application-led growth will define the next phase of the protocol economy.

## Simon Kim — AI and the Expansion of Web3 Development

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 8](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*OinK5ZGmoNKFGFeTQy3PcA.png)

AI has already transformed how code is written. In Web2, 82% of developers use AI tools weekly, generating 41% of all code — 256 billion lines in 2024 alone. This wave is now reaching Web3, where only 24,000 developers contribute monthly — 0.7% of the global base. The gap reflects steep barriers: technical complexity, strict security requirements, and rapidly evolving standards. But as with PCs, the internet, and mobile, every transformative technology eventually democratizes. Web3’s turn is here.

Web3-specialized AI assistants and no-code builders are collapsing these barriers. Natural language converts directly into production-ready smart contracts with embedded security validation — compressing months of Solidity expertise into minutes of interaction. The impact is structural: eliminating both the productivity constraint for developers and the knowledge barrier for newcomers simultaneously. Notion and Canva followed this pattern, reaching multi-billion dollar valuations not by making experts faster, but by making creation accessible. Web3 development is undergoing the same expansion.

This creates three structural investment opportunities.

The first is AI-powered development platforms — the Web3 Figma. These platforms use AI to translate intent into deployable code, debug in real time, and suggest optimizations automatically. They expand the addressable market by orders of magnitude, transforming non-developers into builders through conversational interfaces and visual programming. Early-stage platforms that combine AI generation with intuitive abstractions will capture entire ecosystems through network effects and developer lock-in.

The second layer captures where general platforms become specialized utility. Vertical-specific builders use AI trained on domain-specific patterns to solve actual workflow problems. A DeFi builder understands treasury operations, multi-sig configurations, and yield strategies automatically. An NFT platform recognizes marketplace mechanics, royalty structures, and collection management. These tools embed industry expertise through AI that speaks the language of each vertical, making them faster to adopt and harder to replace. Each major vertical represents a discrete, multi-billion dollar market where AI-powered domain integration creates defensible moats.

The third layer secures this expansion through automated audit platforms. As AI-generated code scales, traditional audits — costing up to $500,000 and taking weeks — become structurally unviable. Cryptocurrency losses exceeded $3.1 billion in the first half of 2025 alone. Automated platforms that validate code at generation speed are not optional tooling — they are essential infrastructure for making mass deployment economically feasible.

The foundation is set: 85% of developers already use AI, cutting development time by 30–60%. With first-mover advantages prevailing, now is the opportunity to invest in teams building market-critical infrastructure. Those who create the tools and those who secure them will shape the future Web3 ecosystem.

## Baek Kim — Privacy Becomes the New Scaling Problem

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 9](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*2hbkexXkQ1byoIzlFs0nwg.png)

By 2026, scalability is no longer the single biggest constraint it once was. L2 throughput has increased over 100x in the past five years, fees have dropped from double-digit dollars to fractions of a cent, and major networks now sustain a few thousands tx/s with production-grade reliability. Stablecoins alone settled US$9T in economic value over the past year, and on-chain RWA markets are already 4x larger than they were two years ago.

But as infrastructure finally works, a deeper limitation emerges: the global economy cannot operate on transparent rails. Financial institutions cannot expose order flow or liquidity positions. Healthcare systems cannot reveal clinical or genomic data. Defense networks cannot leak telemetry or strategic communications. Supply chains and logistics firms cannot expose routing or vendor dependencies. AI agents cannot reveal internal models, prompts, or negotiation logic.

Scalability was a technical bottleneck. Privacy is an _economic, competitive, and geopolitical bottleneck_.

And the rise of AI intensifies the pressure. AI systems demand massive cross-organization data sharing — model inference, scoring, verification, negotiation, multi-agent orchestration — but none of this can move onto public blockchains without confidentiality guarantees. The collision of AI × data collaboration × global coordination makes privacy non-optional.

The research breakthroughs are real:

*   ZKP for verifiable computation, identity, settlement.
*   FHE for encrypted model inference and sensitive data analytics.
*   MPC for multi-party workflows and collaborative computation.
*   zkTLS for trust-preserving bridges between Web2 and Web3.
*   Private execution environments for confidential on-chain state.

But commercialization lags behind.

Most implementations remain prototypes, papers, or closed pilots. The gap between cryptographic possibility and enterprise-grade deployment is now the industry’s largest structural bottleneck.

In 2026, the winners will be the systems that fuse privacy with the programmability and composability of modern blockchains:

*   ZK-native execution layers built for institutional workflows,
*   FHE-compatible smart contract environments,
*   privacy middleware for payments, treasury, and stablecoin rails,
*   confidential compute for AI agents coordinating economically on-chain.

Scalability unlocked institutional interest. Privacy will unlock institutional adoption.

It is the next scaling problem — one that determines whether crypto becomes the coordination substrate for finance, healthcare, defense, logistics, public infrastructure, and the emerging AI-agent economy.

## Dan Park — Stablecoin Rails Become Enterprise Working Capital

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 10](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*XCB3O3ZDatWNHLkBqlm29w.png)

By 2026, infrastructure alone no longer differentiates a team. High-throughput chains, modular stacks, and commoditized validators made “launching an L1/L2” trivial. The real asymmetry — the place where economic depth actually forms — has moved upstream: how stablecoin infrastructure integrates into enterprise cash cycles.

Stablecoins are no longer just payment tokens or trading collateral. They are increasingly behaving like _units of account inside real workflows_:

*   Supplier payments and vendor settlements,
*   FX hedging and intra-firm currency conversion,
*   Inter-entity netting for global subsidiaries,
*   Treasury routing, reconciliation, and end-of-day liquidity positioning.

In these workflows, on-chain liquidity isn’t idle capital anymore. It becomes working capital — cash that moves 24/7 through operational pipelines.

The leading indicator of this shift is _B2B stablecoin settlement_. When stablecoin flows begin to mirror traditional treasury operations — rolling cash sweeps, automated payables/receivables, multi-entity consolidation — adoption is no longer narrative-driven; it is embedded in enterprise infrastructure. This is the signal that matters.

What determines winning teams is not throughput, incentives, or synthetic ecosystems. It is functional depth:

*   Can they move regulated liquidity across jurisdictions?
*   Can they reconcile on-chain flows with ERP, accounting, and compliance systems?
*   Can they close the loop between crypto rails and fintech rails without operational leakage?
*   Can they support enterprise-grade auditability, traceability, and privacy constraints?

Technical novelty only matters when it compounds integration. The defensibility lies in how deeply stablecoins sink into enterprise workflows, not in how fast a chain produces blocks.

As regulation aligns and stablecoin frameworks mature across the U.S., EU, Singapore, Korea, Hong Kong, Abu Dhabi, Japan, and others, enterprises will shift from pilot usage to production integration. Stablecoins will become the transport layer for money — crypto will go invisible but indispensable.

## Ryan Kim — RWAs: The First Real On-Chain Workflows

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 11](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*IlU9qXr_HrBsPvIjwbCyyA.png)

The macro backdrop matters more for RWAs than any other segment. In the U.S., the 10Y Treasury yield holding at 4–4.5%, with a realistic lower bound around 3–3.5%, creates a structural shift: the 0% rate environment that once fueled Bitcoin-style convexity is gone. A 3–4% cost of capital represents a materially different regime for a market that had been conditioned on near-zero liquidity costs. As a result, global flows have begun rotating from high-beta speculation toward assets with audited cash flows and predictable yield.

But the reason RWAs will matter in 2026 is not because they are “yield products.” It’s because they represent the first _real on-chain workflows tied to the external economy_.

Across jurisdictions — the U.S. stablecoin bill, Singapore’s digital asset frameworks, Abu Dhabi’s tokenization regime, Hong Kong’s AMLO/RWA pilots, Japan’s PSA revisions, and Korea’s emerging digital asset oversight — stablecoin regulation and RWA securitization are converging. Together, they will create the first legally recognized pipeline for enterprises to issue credit, settle invoices, manage receivables, and move collateral directly on public chains.

These are not narratives. They are high-frequency economic activities:

*   B2B payments and supplier financing
*   G2P disbursement pilots
*   Collateralized trade finance
*   On-chain reporting and attestations
*   Treasury and settlement operations

As these workflows take root, a new category will form: _Regulated DeFi._

It won’t look like yield farming. It will resemble institutional finance:

(1) Exchanges for tokenized credit and liability instruments

(2) Derivatives for hedging rate, FX, and credit exposure

(3) Credit ratings & analytics tied to on-chain reporting and attestations

(4) Lending markets using real collateral and predictable cash flows

This stack doesn’t replace TradFi infrastructure — it connects to it, using stablecoins as the liquidity substrate and RWAs as the bridge to real activity. The combination creates a feedback loop:

*   Stablecoins supply the liquidity base,
*   RWAs create demand anchored in real economic cycles,
*   Regulated DeFi provides markets, hedging, and leverage.

RWA is not the endgame, nor is it “bringing bonds on-chain.” It will be simply the first category where real production, commerce, and credit cycles touch public blockchains at scale. It proves that crypto can power workflows the real economy depends on, not just speculative loops.

In 2026, the question won’t be whether RWAs grow — they will. The question will be which teams can align regulation, origination, credit expertise, and settlement infrastructure into repeatable, compliant systems. Those will be the builders who will define the first institutional markets of the protocol economy.

## SJ Baek — Tokenized Private Credit: The First Scalable On-Chain Yield Market

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 12](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*CWqYmN0uh05MTBkh50wxQA.png)

As stablecoin usage accelerates across payments and enterprise settlement, a growing pool of on-chain balances is searching for yield beyond treasuries and money-market funds. The most natural destination is private credit — a US$3T TradFi asset class with attractive risk-adjusted returns and consistent institutional demand.

And private credit is emerging as the first _fixed-income category with real potential to scale on-chain_. By 2026, penetration is projected to reach low single-digit percentages, which may sound modest but represents one of the fastest institutional migrations into digital markets to date. The reason is structural: tokenization directly resolves the three frictions that have constrained private credit for decades:

1.   Low liquidity → fractionalized, tradable credit tokens
2.   High operational cost → on-chain servicing, automation, programmable reporting
3.   Opaque disclosure → real-time collateral records and transparent repayment flows

When these frictions collapse, private credit becomes technically scalable and interoperable — but technical scalability alone doesn’t create real credit markets.

Technical scalability unlocks the category, but market scalability depends on something else: deal origination — the ability to source, diligence, price, and structure credit the way top private lenders do. Winning platforms will combine:

*   Institutional-grade underwriting,
*   Regulatory alignment,
*   Cross-jurisdiction issuance capability,
*   And interoperability with stablecoin settlement rails.

As the global stablecoin float grows, a predictable portion of that liquidity may migrate into yield-bearing, securitized private credit, not because it’s “financial,” but because it is the first on-chain product with repeatable, risk-priced cash flows.

Tokenized private credit is not the end state of on-chain fixed income. It is simply the first scalable market, the category that proves on-chain capital formation can operate with institutional discipline. In 2026, this segment will become a cornerstone of the emerging protocol economy — linking stablecoin liquidity with real-world yield in a transparent, regulated, and composable structure.

## Edward Tan — ETH/BTC as the Market’s Risk Cycle Compass

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 13](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*sJh0unn7_oeEVFCYYYvV3A.png)

Across many major cycles, one ratio has tended to map the market’s risk appetite more consistently than narratives, sector rotations, or social sentiment: ETH/BTC. What matters is not the absolute level but the persistence of ETH strength relative to BTC — measured in consecutive “green weeks” — which has historically often preceded expansions in liquidity breadth and downstream risk-taking.

The pattern has appeared surprisingly stable across cycles.

*   In 2020, ETH/BTC rose from 0.02 → 0.03 over 4–5 consecutive green weeks, triggering the DeFi 1.0 rally across UNI, AAVE, YFI, and LDO.
*   In early 2021, the ratio climbed from 0.03 → 0.045 over a similar streak, setting up the broad alt season.
*   From 2021–22, it moved 0.055 → 0.08 during a 3–4 week window before the Luna/3AC unwind.
*   In late 2024, following the U.S. election, 0.03 → 0.04 over 3 weeks preceded rotation into PENDLE, ENA, and restaking names.
*   In May–Sep 2025, a higher low at 0.022 followed by a reclaim of 0.025 — driven by ETH ETF inflows and rising ETH-denominated DAT activity — set up strength in SOL, ENA, and HYPE.

These episodes share a similar structure: the market rotates before anyone “feels” it, and the earliest recurring signal has often been ETH/BTC showing sustained relative strength.

The counterexample — 2023 — reinforces the rule. In that BTC-dominated year, ETH/BTC slid from 0.077 → 0.05 with no momentum streaks, and altcoins barely participated despite BTC’s rally. It appeared to act as a risk-off indicator, even before sentiment recognized it.

This ratio may remain relevant in 2026 even as the market becomes more institutional and liquidity is distributed across more ecosystems. Rotations may be shorter or more selective, but the behavioral mechanics — flow concentration → liquidity breadth → narrative expansion — are unchanged. ETH/BTC still tends to indicate when capital is preparing to move down the risk curve.

Once that signal appears, the confirmation has in several cycles come from on-chain fee capture. Protocols with high revenue-to-TVL ratios — Uniswap, Lido, AAVE in 2020–21; GMX and Pendle in 2023–24; Hyperliquid, Syrup, and Euler in 2024–25 — have often corresponded with outperforming cohorts. These ecosystems also tend to be the data-rich ones (Ethereum, Solana, Base, Arbitrum), where fundamentals can be observed in real time rather than inferred.

Together, these two indicators form a practical compass: ETH/BTC persistence signals when risk appetite is broadening; fee capture reveals where it is justified. When momentum fades, one reasonable approach has historically been reverting to majors and cash. When it persists, opportunity lies in ecosystems where revenue robustness confirms the rotation.

This is not a price model — it is a descriptive framework of how crypto has historically reallocated risk. And in 2026, as capital becomes more selective and fundamentals matter more, this map becomes even more valuable.

## SB An — Crypto as a Real-Time Speculation Markets

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 14](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*gfYews74FBFgqqr40mTaxg.png)

One of the most overlooked shifts entering 2026 is not technological but cognitive. Users no longer think of crypto as a consensus network or a distributed ledger — those abstractions have faded into the background. With stablecoins functioning as intuitive digital money, account abstraction hiding key-management complexity, and execution layers approaching real-time performance, the mental model has changed: crypto has become a 24/7 Speculation Markets, a place where people take views, place risk, observe results immediately, and iterate.

This shift is rooted in human behavior. Neuroscience shows that dopamine spikes are driven by reward prediction error — the gap between expected and actual outcomes. Traditional finance cannot fully engage this loop: limited trading hours, multi-day settlement, and delayed feedback break the action→resolution cycle. Crypto collapses that delay. Continuous markets, instant settlement, and programmable payoff structures make the entire system architecturally optimized for high-frequency prediction behavior. Users form expectations, resolve uncertainty in seconds rather than days, and re-enter the loop. Once experienced, this feedback cycle produces persistent, path-dependent demand — a speculation market that did not and could not exist on traditional rails.

We already see this taking shape through distinct product verticals along the risk spectrum. Perpetual DEXs provide leveraged, continuous directional exposure; prediction markets offer binary or probabilistic outcomes tied to real-world events; TCG-style economic systems experiment with randomized reward mechanics. Though the surface experiences differ, they all rely on the same neuropsychological substrate: immediate resolution, tight expectation loops, and continuous engagement.

But these markets cannot scale without solving three structural bottlenecks.

First is the oracle problem. Price-feed oracles work for crypto-native assets, but real-world event settlement — elections, weather, earnings — requires a mechanism that combines truth discovery, dispute resolution, and economic security. In high-stakes markets, the incentive to manipulate the oracle can exceed the protocol’s security budget, turning this into a mechanism-design challenge, not merely a technical one.

Second is the liquidity cold start. Early markets face adverse selection, inventory risk, high impermanent loss, and insufficient fee income to compensate LPs. Traditional market-making models do not work here. Protocols need new economic structures — restaking-secured incentives, probabilistic yield layers, or multi-tiered reward curves — to bootstrap liquidity without collapsing the risk-return equation for early participants.

Third is privacy. Transparent ledgers expose strategy, position size, behavioral patterns, and even timing preferences — creating vectors for MEV extraction, social engineering, and targeted adversarial behavior. Zero-knowledge proofs, FHE, and confidential execution can mitigate this, but integration at the application layer remains early, and the tension between privacy and composability is unresolved. This tension is now one of the core constraints for scalable speculation markets

What makes 2026 unique is that these challenges are becoming solvable simultaneously. Scalable privacy primitives, agent-driven settlement, and increasingly robust oracle architectures are converging with user behavior that already treats crypto as an always-on expectation engine. The result is not Web2 markets rebuilt on-chain, but entirely new market forms — continuous, permissionless, composable systems where humans and agents express probabilistic beliefs in real time.

Crypto is the only programmable infrastructure capable of supporting this economic form at scale. And once the speculation markets take root, it becomes irreversible: a new category of demand that persists regardless of market cycles. The frontier ahead is not about mimicking traditional finance — it is about enabling economic behaviors that only crypto rails can support.

## Appendix — 2025 Regulatory Convergence: Institutional Integration of Digital Assets by Hashed Open Research

_2025 marked a global shift from fragmented oversight to calibrated regulatory frameworks, establishing the conditions for institutional participation._

Across Hashed’s key jurisdictions, including the United States, Korea, Japan, India, Southeast Asia and the United Arab Emirates, authorities moved from establishing minimum safeguards to operational implementation. Regulatory frameworks aligned national interests with standards governing issuance, custody, and settlement.

Central banks and international standard-setting bodies complemented these efforts by shifting from exploratory research to early policy integration, beginning to position digital assets within the architecture of monetary and financial infrastructure. Pilot programs, regulatory sandboxes, and cross-border initiatives created the technical and legal foundations for interoperability, strengthening regulatory certainty and enabling institutional confidence and large-scale liquidity formation.

## The State of Crypto Regulation 2025

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 15](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*GtepFqZyksSmO4ZkqwPavA.png)

The sections that follow review the regulatory evolution of 2025 across major jurisdictions, with primary focus on the United States, Korea, and the United Arab Emirates. Developments in other key markets are referenced only briefly, in line with the scope and audience of this reading. The discussion is organized around three core themes (1) stablecoin settlement, (2) institutional custody, and (3) the tokenization of real-world assets, offering an overview of the regulatory trends that define 2025.

*This reading uses “crypto”, “digital assets” and “virtual assets” interchangeably, except where specific legal definitions require distinction.

## United States

## Overview — Toward a Coherent Federal Framework for Digital Assets

In 2025, the United States entered a more permissive regulatory environment for institutional engagement with digital assets, as agencies began withdrawing earlier constraints and replacing them with clearer supervisory frameworks. The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), and Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) reversed or softened prior guidance that had created operational barriers for financial institutions, while the President’s Working Group on Digital Asset Markets advanced recommendations aimed at strengthening U.S. leadership in the sector. This shift included the SEC’s retreat from a series of enforcement actions broadly viewed as “regulation by enforcement,” alongside parallel moves by other regulators to open the door to greater participation by traditional financial institutions. Against this evolving policy landscape, North America already constitutes the largest share of high-value crypto flows, accounting for 45% of all transaction value in transfers exceeding $10 million ([Chainalysis](https://www.chainalysis.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/the-2025-geography-of-crypto-report-release.pdf), 2025).

The regulatory recalibration of 2025 reflected a broader strategic aim: to keep the expanding digital asset economy within U.S. oversight and, in doing so, sustain the dollar’s primacy by positioning U.S.-regulated, dollar-denominated assets as a central destination for global liquidity.

Formalization of Stablecoin Settlement

The passage of the GENIUS Act (S.1582) established a comprehensive federal framework for privately issued, fiat-backed stablecoins. The Act not only institutionalized stablecoin settlement but also anchored global digital-dollar liquidity to U.S. sovereign markets by requiring full backing in Treasuries and other high-quality liquid assets. Across market participants, payment networks and fintech firms moved to embed regulated stablecoins into core rails with Visa launching a settlement pilot supporting stablecoins, Stripe expanding USDC payouts globally, and PayPal’s PYUSD securing strategic exchange-partnerships, widening access for institutions and merchants.

Institutionalization of Custody

Regulatory constraints that long limited institutional crypto custody were also removed. The repeal of SAB 121 through [SAB 122](https://www.sec.gov/rules-regulations/staff-guidance/staff-accounting-bulletins/staff-accounting-bulletin-122), together with complementary SEC and [OCC guidance](https://www.occ.gov/news-issuances/news-releases/2025/nr-occ-2025-42.html), clarified capital treatment and custody permissions, enabling U.S. banks and funds to manage digital assets at scale. Major custodians, including BNY Mellon, State Street, and Fidelity, along with asset managers such as BlackRock and Franklin Templeton, expanded their on-chain issuance, collateral management, and settlement services. This maturation redefined custody from a compliance risk to a regulated financial service.

Tokenization of Yield and Real World Assets

Joint actions by the SEC and CFTC established conditions for the trading of tokenized Treasuries, money market funds, and futures, integrating real world assets (RWAs) into regulated financial markets. Institutional inflows accrued to tokenized yield instruments, with total value locked in tokenized real world assets (RWA) markets reaching approximately $35 billion ([RWA.xyz](http://rwa.xyz/) dashboard, Nov 2025), representing roughly a 133% year-to-date increase, marking the strongest growth cycle on record.

## Key Developments — Codifying the Federal Architecture of Digital Asset Oversight

Three landmark legislative actions defined the foundation of U.S. digital asset policy in 2025, culminating in the July ‘Crypto Week.’ The GENIUS Act (S.1582), signed into law on July 18, established the first federal framework for stablecoin issuance. The CLARITY Act (H.R.3633), passed by the House, addressed long-standing jurisdictional overlap between the SEC and CFTC by specifying how digital assets transition from securities to commodities. The Anti-CBDC Act (H.R.1919) prohibited the Federal Reserve from issuing a retail central bank digital currency, reaffirming the private sector’s role in the development and circulation of digital dollars.

Collectively, these measures established a regulatory model in which the state defines the perimeter of oversight while the private sector leads in issuance and operational deployment. Complementing this legislative framework, the introduction of IRS Form 1099-DA, effective for the 2025 tax year, brought digital asset reporting into alignment with equity market disclosure standards, advancing regulatory consistency and institutional compliance.

Overall, 2025 established the legal and institutional architecture necessary for the United States to move toward coordinated supervision. The challenge now lies in executing these frameworks with the consistency and prudential rigor expected of the world’s primary financial jurisdiction.

## Korea

## Overview — Establishing a Dual-Track Framework for Digital Asset Regulation

Korea has become one of the world’s most active and digitally advanced markets for digital assets. With a population highly accustomed to digital finance and among the fastest internet infrastructures globally, crypto adoption has expanded beyond retail interest into mainstream finance and policy. In 2025, digital assets emerged as a prominent component of the national economic agenda, reflected in the new administration’s policy commitments and growing participation by major banks and fintech firms.

Historically, Korea’s regulatory foundation rested on the Capital Markets Act, which narrowly defined “investment contract securities” and left most non-security tokens outside its scope. To address this gap, authorities adopted a dual-track strategy, maintaining the Capital Markets Act for security-type tokens while introducing a distinct framework for non-securities. This approach culminated in the Act on the Protection of Virtual Asset Users, which took effect in July 2024, the country’s first comprehensive law governing digital assets. The Act established baseline investor protections, including the segregation of user assets and prohibitions on unfair trading practices.

Korea’s legislative trajectory now aligns more closely with the European Union’s MiCA (Markets in Crypto Assets Regulation) framework than with the U.S. model. In the United States, the broad application of the Howey Test captures most tokenized assets as securities, resulting in overlapping jurisdiction between the SEC and CFTC. By contrast, Korea’s Capital Markets Act defines investment contract securities more narrowly, leaving non-security tokens outside its scope. To close this regulatory gap, Korea adopted an approach similar to the EU, enacting a separate Virtual Asset Act to govern non-securities while maintaining existing securities laws for tokenized financial instruments. The first phase of this framework primarily addressed secondary-market risks, with future legislation expected to establish standards for issuance, disclosure, and business conduct.

## Key Developments — Implementing Phase 2

Having established Phase 1 through its dual-track foundation and Virtual Asset Act, Korea advanced Phase 2 in 2025, moving toward implementation, supervision, and issuance standards. Debates in the National Assembly centered on three priority areas: establishing issuance and disclosure requirements for token offerings (ICOs), expanding the scope and conduct standards of virtual asset providers (VASPs), and introducing conflict-of-interest rules separating brokerage, listing and custody functions within exchanges. These initiatives aim to extend investor protection from secondary market activity to the full lifecycle of digital asset issuance and trading.

The year also marked the formal beginning of Korea’s stablecoin policy agenda. Drawing conceptually from the U.S. GENIUS Act, proposed bills by Representatives Min Byung-deok, An Do-geol, and Kim Eun-hye outlined a licensing regime under the Financial Services Commission (FSC), full-reserve requirements, and enhanced audit and disclosure obligations. In parallel, KB Kookmin Bank and several other financial institutions launched KRW-backed stablecoin pilots within the FSC’s Financial Regulatory Sandbox, reflecting regulatory openness to controlled experimentation under prudential supervision.

Overall, Korea’s bipartisan endorsement of the emerging “Digital G2” agenda in 2025, coined by Hashed Open Research, reflects a coordinated effort to position the country’s digital asset landscape as an instrument of global leadership. The immediate policy task is to achieve regulatory coherence among the Capital Markets Act, the Virtual Asset Act, and forthcoming stablecoin legislation, ensuring consistency as Korea transitions from reactive oversight to a coordinated framework capable of sustaining institutional participation.

Notably, these developments are unfolding alongside Korea’s drive toward ‘AI G3,’ a national initiative aimed at positioning the country alongside the United States and China in the global AI competition. In 2025, BlackRock CEO Larry Fink publicly stated his intention to help establish Korea as the ‘AI capital of Asia,’ while NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang announced a commitment to supply approximately 260,000 Blackwell processors, an investment of up to USD 10 billion, to support the country’s AI infrastructure. As Korea accelerates its AI build-out, its digital asset ambitions are gaining complementary momentum. With AI G3 and Digital G2 reinforcing each other, the growing AI-driven demand for compute, data, and application layers is likely to increase the need for tokenized infrastructure, stable settlement rails, and regulated digital asset markets. Taken together, these parallel tracks position Korea as an emerging core region in Asia’s digital and technological economy, making forthcoming regulatory and market developments particularly consequential.

## United Arab Emirates (Dubai and Abu Dhabi Global Markets)

## Overview — Consolidating Federal and Emirate-Level Regulatory Mandates

The United Arab Emirates entered 2025 in a phase of regulatory consolidation, advancing from its innovation-led flexibility to institutionally grounded supervisory framework. The year focused on tightening regulation, reinforcing accountability and formalizing supervisory mandates across the region’s focal points, Dubai and Abu Dhabi.

Virtual asset regulation is administered at the federal level by the Securities and Commodities Authority while Dubai’s Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority (VARA) operates under delegated authority and Abu Dhabi’s Financial Services Regulatory Authority (FSRA) maintains independent jurisdiction within the Abu Dhabi Global Markets (ADGM). Together, these bodies constitute a coordinated regulatory architecture that integrates federal policy direction with regional specialization.

The impact has been measurable. In the first half of 2025, the SCA reported a 55% increase in newly issued licenses and a 60% rise in licensed entities year-on-year. Foreign investment funds expanded by 54%, and the value of registered bond and sukuk programs grew by 35% ([SCA](https://www.sca.gov.ae/en/media-center/news/14/7/2025/sca-board-reports-record-first-half-milestones-and-initiates), July 2025), reflecting stronger institutional engagement.

## Key Developments — VARA Rulebook 2.0 and FSRA Consultations on Fiat Referenced Tokens and Staking

Dubai — Virtual Asset Regulatory Authority (VARA)

In Dubai (excluding the Dubai International Financial Centre), 2025 marked a decisive regulatory inflection point as VARA introduced Rulebook 2.0, transitioning from a focus on licensing fundamentals to operational governance. The updated framework established a dedicated regime for Asset-Referenced Virtual Assets and tokenized real world assets, clarified licensing categories and issuance pathways for virtual asset service providers, and introduced stricter requirements on leverage, custody, and disclosure, particularly for retail participants. The update signaled VARA’s progression from facilitating market entry to supervising market maturity, reflecting the UAE’s intent to develop differentiated regulatory regimes for distinct segments of the digital asset economy.

Abu Dhabi Global Markets (ADGM) — Financial Services Regulatory Authority (FSRA)

In ADGM, the FSRA expanded the institutional perimeter of its digital asset regime through a series of targeted [consultations](https://www.adgm.com/legal-framework/public-consultations). Consultation Paper №9 (2025) proposed a comprehensive framework for Fiat-Referenced Tokens (FRTs), introducing an “Accepted FRT” designation and expanding oversight of custodians and issuers. Consultation Paper №10 (2025) set out standards for virtual asset staking, defining intermediary categories authorized to hold or control client assets and imposing detailed governance and client asset protection measures. These initiatives mark a shift from basic exchange and custody oversight toward recognizing and regulating the more advanced service layers of the tokenized economy.

As [noted by Carnegie](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2025/05/the-future-of-cryptocurrency-in-the-gulf-cooperation-council-countries?lang=en) (May 2025), the Gulf’s engagement with digital assets has been driven by “economic reform, the pursuit of financial sovereignty, and resilience in the face of geopolitical shifts and oil-driven vulnerabilities.” The UAE’s continued leadership in 2025, grounded in deliberate regulatory design, reflects this intent and shows how proactive policymaking can convert technological innovation into lasting economic strength and institutional credibility.
